self_declared_problem_solved:
Let $T$ denote a finite, weighted tree with vertex set $V$, edge set $E$, and weight function $w : V \longrightarrow \bZ$.
Let $L(T)$ denote the free abelian group generated by $V$, equipped with the symmetric, bilinear form $\cdot$ whose value on a pair of generators $u,v \in V$ is given by 
\[
u \cdot v = 
\begin{cases}
    w(u), & u=v; \\
    -1, & (u,v) \in E; \textup{ and} \\
    0, & u \ne v, (u,v) \notin E.
\end{cases}
\]
Suppose that the form is positive definite, and suppose that there exists a single vertex $v$ for which the vertex weight $w(v)$ is less than the vertex degree $d(v)$.

Prove that $T$ contains a vertex $u$ such that $u$ is an irreducible element of $L(T)$, i.e.~there do not exist nonzero lattice elements $a,b \in L(T)$ such that $u = a+b$ and $a \cdot b \ge 0$.

description: writeup (full problem): Let T be a finite weighted tree with positive-definite lattice form. Suppose there is a unique vertex b, equivalently a single vertex, such that w(b) < d_T(b). Then T contains a vertex u that is irreducible in L(T).
full_text:
## Original Problem

Let $T$ denote a finite, weighted tree with vertex set $V$, edge set $E$, and weight function $w : V \longrightarrow \bZ$.
Let $L(T)$ denote the free abelian group generated by $V$, equipped with the symmetric, bilinear form $\cdot$ whose value on a pair of generators $u,v \in V$ is given by 
\[
u \cdot v = 
\begin{cases}
    w(u), & u=v; \\
    -1, & (u,v) \in E; \textup{ and} \\
    0, & u \ne v, (u,v) \notin E.
\end{cases}
\]
Suppose that the form is positive definite, and suppose that there exists a single vertex $v$ for which the vertex weight $w(v)$ is less than the vertex degree $d(v)$.

Prove that $T$ contains a vertex $u$ such that $u$ is an irreducible element of $L(T)$, i.e.~there do not exist nonzero lattice elements $a,b \in L(T)$ such that $u = a+b$ and $a \cdot b \ge 0$.

## Problem Solved

We prove the following fully self-contained statement.

Let \(T=(V,E)\) be a finite tree, let \(w:V\to\mathbb Z\) be an integer weight function, and let \(d_T(v)\) denote the degree of a vertex \(v\) in \(T\). Let
\[
L(T)=\bigoplus_{v\in V}\mathbb Z v
\]
be the free abelian group generated by the vertices of \(T\), equipped with the symmetric bilinear form determined by
\[
u\cdot u=w(u),\qquad
u\cdot v=-1\text{ if }u\ne v\text{ are adjacent},\qquad
u\cdot v=0\text{ otherwise}.
\]
Assume this form is positive definite. Assume there is exactly one vertex \(b\in V\) such that
\[
w(b)<d_T(b).
\]
Call an element \(y\in L(T)\) irreducible if there do not exist nonzero \(a,c\in L(T)\) with
\[
y=a+c
\quad\text{and}\quad
a\cdot c\ge 0.
\]
Then \(T\) contains an irreducible vertex. More precisely, every weight-one vertex is irreducible; if \(T\) has no weight-one vertex, then the unique vertex \(b\) with \(w(b)<d_T(b)\) is irreducible.

This is exactly the original problem with “a single vertex” understood in its literal uniqueness sense, so it is not a relaxation.

## Proof

For \(x\in L(T)\), write \(x^2=x\cdot x\).

### 1. Positive integral norms and weight-one vertices

If
\[
x=\sum_{v\in V} n_v v,\qquad n_v\in\mathbb Z,
\]
then
\[
x^2=\sum_{v\in V} n_v^2 w(v)-2\sum_{\{u,v\}\in E} n_u n_v.
\]
Thus \(x^2\in\mathbb Z\). Since the form is positive definite, every nonzero \(x\in L(T)\) satisfies
\[
x^2\in\mathbb Z_{>0}.
\]

Now suppose \(u\in V\) has \(w(u)=1\). If
\[
u=a+c,
\qquad a,c\ne 0,
\qquad a\cdot c\ge 0,
\]
then \(a^2,c^2\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\), so
\[
1=u^2=(a+c)^2=a^2+c^2+2a\cdot c\ge 1+1=2,
\]
a contradiction. Therefore every weight-one vertex is irreducible.

So from now on it remains only to prove that if there are no weight-one vertices, then the unique bad vertex \(b\) is irreducible.

### 2. A Schur-complement fact

We will repeatedly use the following elementary fact. Let
\[
M=
\begin{pmatrix}
a & c^T\\
c & B
\end{pmatrix},
\]
where \(B\) is symmetric positive definite. Define
\[
S=a-c^T B^{-1}c.
\]
Then
\[
M=
\begin{pmatrix}
1 & c^T B^{-1}\\
0 & I
\end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix}
S & 0\\
0 & B
\end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix}
1 & 0\\
B^{-1}c & I
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Hence \(M\) is positive definite if and only if \(S>0\). When \(S>0\),
\[
M^{-1}=
\begin{pmatrix}
S^{-1} & -S^{-1}c^TB^{-1}\\
-B^{-1}cS^{-1} & B^{-1}+B^{-1}cS^{-1}c^TB^{-1}
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

### 3. The rooted nonbad branch lemma

Let \(C\) be a finite weighted tree rooted at a vertex \(r\). Let \(d_C(v)\) denote degree inside \(C\). Let \(A\) be the Gram matrix of \(C\), written in the vertex basis, and assume \(A\) is positive definite.

Assume the rooted nonbad inequalities
\[
w(r)\ge d_C(r)+1,
\qquad
w(u)\ge d_C(u)\quad\text{for every }u\ne r.
\]
Let
\[
G=A^{-1},
\qquad
e_r=\text{the coordinate vector of }r,
\qquad
\alpha=e_r^TGe_r=(A^{-1})_{rr}.
\]

We prove:

1. \(0<\alpha\le 1\).
2. If \(\lambda\in\mathbb Z^C\) has odd root coordinate and even nonroot coordinates, meaning
   \[
   \lambda_r\equiv 1\pmod 2,
   \qquad
   \lambda_u\equiv 0\pmod 2\quad(u\ne r),
   \]
   then
   \[
   \lambda^TG\lambda\ge \alpha,
   \]
   with equality if and only if \(\lambda=\pm e_r\).
3. For every \(x\in\mathbb Z^C\), if \(c=x_r\) is the root coefficient and
   \[
   Q=x^TAx
   \]
   is its branch norm, then for every \(k\in\mathbb Z\),
   \[
   (2k-1)c-Q\le k(k-1)\alpha.
   \]
   Equality occurs only when
   \[
   x=kGe_r
   \quad\text{or}\quad
   x=(k-1)Ge_r,
   \]
   with the displayed vector integral.
4. If \(k=0\) or \(k=1\), equality with \(x\ne 0\) forces \(C\) to contain a weight-one vertex.

#### Proof of the dual-parity inequality

We prove parts 1 and 2 by induction on \(|C|\).

If \(C=\{r\}\), then \(A=[w(r)]\). Since \(w(r)\ge d_C(r)+1=1\),
\[
\alpha=\frac1{w(r)}\le 1.
\]
For an odd integer \(s\),
\[
s^2\alpha\ge \alpha,
\]
with equality exactly when \(s=\pm1\). Thus the claim holds for one vertex.

Now assume \(|C|>1\). Let \(C_1,\dots,C_m\) be the components of \(C-r\). Root \(C_i\) at the vertex \(r_i\) adjacent to \(r\). Let \(A_i\) be the Gram matrix of \(C_i\), let
\[
G_i=A_i^{-1},
\qquad
e_i=e_{r_i},
\qquad
\alpha_i=e_i^TG_ie_i.
\]
Each \(C_i\) again satisfies the rooted nonbad hypotheses: its root \(r_i\) has lost the edge to \(r\), so
\[
w(r_i)\ge d_C(r_i)=d_{C_i}(r_i)+1,
\]
and all other vertices have the same degree in \(C_i\) as in \(C\). Therefore, by induction,
\[
0<\alpha_i\le 1.
\]

Order the vertices of \(C\) by putting \(r\) first and then the vertices of \(C_1,\dots,C_m\). Then
\[
A=
\begin{pmatrix}
w(r) & -\eta^T\\
-\eta & B
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
B=A_1\oplus\cdots\oplus A_m,
\]
where the \(C_i\)-component of \(\eta\) is \(e_i\). The Schur complement is
\[
S=w(r)-\eta^TB^{-1}\eta
  =w(r)-\sum_i \alpha_i.
\]
Since \(A\) is positive definite, \(S>0\), and
\[
\alpha=(A^{-1})_{rr}=\frac1S.
\]
Also, because \(m=d_C(r)\),
\[
S=w(r)-\sum_i\alpha_i
\ge (m+1)-m
=1.
\]
Thus
\[
0<\alpha=\frac1S\le 1.
\]

Now let \(\lambda\in\mathbb Z^C\) have odd root coordinate and even nonroot coordinates. Write
\[
\lambda=(s,2\mu_1,\dots,2\mu_m),
\]
where \(s\) is odd and \(\mu_i\in\mathbb Z^{C_i}\). Define
\[
a_i=e_i^TG_i\mu_i,
\qquad
E_i=\mu_i^TG_i\mu_i,
\qquad
A_0=\sum_i a_i.
\]
Using the inverse formula from the Schur complement,
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
=
4\sum_i E_i+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}.
\]

For each \(i\), the two vectors
\[
2\mu_i-e_i
\quad\text{and}\quad
2\mu_i+e_i
\]
have odd root coordinate and even nonroot coordinates in \(C_i\). Applying the induction hypothesis to \(C_i\) gives
\[
(2\mu_i-e_i)^TG_i(2\mu_i-e_i)\ge \alpha_i,
\]
and
\[
(2\mu_i+e_i)^TG_i(2\mu_i+e_i)\ge \alpha_i.
\]
Expanding these inequalities gives
\[
4E_i-4a_i+\alpha_i\ge \alpha_i,
\qquad
4E_i+4a_i+\alpha_i\ge \alpha_i.
\]
Therefore
\[
E_i\ge |a_i|.
\]
Hence
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
\ge
4\sum_i |a_i|+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}
\ge
4|A_0|+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}.
\]

It remains to prove the scalar inequality
\[
4|A_0|+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}\ge \frac1S
\]
for \(S\ge1\) and odd \(s\). Put \(y=2A_0\). After multiplying by \(S\), the desired inequality becomes
\[
(s+y)^2+2S|y|\ge 1.
\]
If \(sy\ge0\), then
\[
|s+y|\ge |s|\ge1,
\]
so the inequality holds. If \(sy<0\), set \(a=|s|\ge1\) and \(u=|y|>0\). Then the left-hand side is
\[
(a-u)^2+2Su.
\]
If \(a=1\), this equals
\[
1+u^2+2(S-1)u>1.
\]
If \(a\ge3\), then either \(u\le a-1\), in which case \((a-u)^2\ge1\), or \(u>a-1\), in which case \(2Su>2(a-1)\ge4\). Thus the scalar inequality always holds. Equality occurs only when
\[
y=0
\quad\text{and}\quad
s=\pm1,
\]
that is, only when
\[
A_0=0
\quad\text{and}\quad
s=\pm1.
\]

Therefore
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda\ge \frac1S=\alpha.
\]
If equality holds, then the scalar equality forces \(A_0=0\) and \(s=\pm1\). In that case
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
=
4\sum_i E_i+\frac1S.
\]
Equality with \(\alpha=1/S\) forces every \(E_i=0\). Since each \(G_i\) is positive definite, \(\mu_i=0\) for every \(i\). Hence
\[
\lambda=\pm e_r.
\]
Conversely, \(\lambda=\pm e_r\) plainly gives equality. This proves the dual-parity inequality.

#### Derivation of the branch inequality

Let \(x\in\mathbb Z^C\). Write
\[
c=x_r,
\qquad
Q=x^TAx.
\]
Fix \(k\in\mathbb Z\), and set
\[
t=2k-1.
\]
Define
\[
\lambda=2Ax-te_r.
\]
Because \(A\) is integral, \(\lambda\in\mathbb Z^C\). Its root coordinate is odd, and every nonroot coordinate is even. Therefore the dual-parity inequality gives
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda\ge \alpha.
\]
Expanding,
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
=
(2Ax-te_r)^TG(2Ax-te_r)
=
4Q-4tc+t^2\alpha.
\]
Thus
\[
4Q-4tc+t^2\alpha\ge \alpha,
\]
so
\[
tc-Q\le \frac{t^2-1}{4}\alpha.
\]
Since \(t=2k-1\),
\[
\frac{t^2-1}{4}=k(k-1).
\]
Therefore
\[
(2k-1)c-Q\le k(k-1)\alpha.
\]

Equality occurs precisely when \(\lambda=\pm e_r\). If \(\lambda=e_r\), then
\[
2Ax-(2k-1)e_r=e_r,
\]
so
\[
Ax=ke_r,
\qquad
x=kGe_r.
\]
If \(\lambda=-e_r\), then
\[
2Ax-(2k-1)e_r=-e_r,
\]
so
\[
Ax=(k-1)e_r,
\qquad
x=(k-1)Ge_r.
\]
Thus equality forces one of the two stated alternatives, and conversely either integral alternative gives equality.

#### The \(k=0,1\) equality analysis

Assume \(k=0\) or \(k=1\), and suppose equality holds with \(x\ne0\). Let
\[
y=Ge_r.
\]
If \(k=1\), the equality alternatives are
\[
x=y
\quad\text{or}\quad
x=0.
\]
If \(k=0\), they are
\[
x=0
\quad\text{or}\quad
x=-y.
\]
Since \(x\ne0\), in either case \(y\in\mathbb Z^C\). Therefore its root coordinate
\[
y_r=\alpha
\]
is a positive integer. But \(0<\alpha\le1\), so
\[
\alpha=1.
\]

We now show that \(\alpha=1\) forces \(C\) to contain a weight-one vertex. Using the notation from the induction step,
\[
\alpha=\frac1S,
\qquad
S=w(r)-\sum_i\alpha_i.
\]
Thus \(\alpha=1\) gives \(S=1\). But
\[
S=w(r)-\sum_i\alpha_i
\ge w(r)-m
\ge 1.
\]
Hence equality holds throughout. If \(m=0\), then \(S=w(r)=1\), so \(r\) has weight one. If \(m>0\), then equality forces every child branch to have \(\alpha_i=1\). Repeating the same argument down a child branch and using finiteness, we eventually reach a one-vertex branch whose root has weight \(1\). Therefore \(C\) contains a weight-one vertex.

This completes the rooted nonbad branch lemma.

### 4. Deleting the unique bad vertex

Let \(b\) be the unique vertex of \(T\) satisfying
\[
w(b)<d_T(b).
\]
Let \(C\) range over the connected components of \(T-b\). For each component \(C\), let \(r_C\) be the unique vertex of \(C\) adjacent to \(b\). Let \(A_C\) be the Gram matrix of \(C\), and define
\[
\alpha_C=(A_C^{-1})_{r_Cr_C}.
\]

Because the full form on \(T\) is positive definite, each \(A_C\), being a principal submatrix, is positive definite.

Because \(b\) is the unique bad vertex, every \(u\ne b\) satisfies
\[
w(u)\ge d_T(u).
\]
For the root \(r_C\),
\[
d_C(r_C)=d_T(r_C)-1,
\]
so
\[
w(r_C)\ge d_T(r_C)=d_C(r_C)+1.
\]
For every nonroot vertex \(u\in C\),
\[
d_C(u)=d_T(u),
\]
so
\[
w(u)\ge d_C(u).
\]
Thus every component \(C\), rooted at \(r_C\), satisfies the rooted nonbad hypotheses of the branch lemma.

Now order the basis of \(L(T)\) by putting \(b\) first, followed by the vertices of the components \(C\). The full Gram matrix has block form
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
w(b) & -\eta^T\\
-\eta & B
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
B=\bigoplus_C A_C,
\]
where the \(C\)-component of \(\eta\) is the coordinate vector \(e_{r_C}\). Its Schur complement at \(b\) is
\[
w(b)-\eta^TB^{-1}\eta
=
w(b)-\sum_C \alpha_C.
\]
Since the full form is positive definite, this Schur complement is positive. Hence
\[
\sum_C \alpha_C<w(b).
\]

### 5. Final calculation: irreducibility of \(b\) when there is no weight-one vertex

Assume now that \(T\) has no weight-one vertex. We prove that \(b\) is irreducible.

It is enough to prove that for every \(x\in L(T)\) with
\[
x\ne0
\quad\text{and}\quad
x\ne b,
\]
one has
\[
x\cdot(b-x)<0.
\]
Indeed, if \(b=a+c\) with \(a,c\ne0\), then taking \(x=a\) gives \(c=b-x\).

Write \(x\) uniquely as
\[
x=kb+\sum_C x_C,
\]
where \(k\in\mathbb Z\) is the coefficient of \(b\), and \(x_C\in L(C)\). For each component \(C\), let
\[
c_C=\text{the coefficient of }r_C\text{ in }x_C,
\qquad
Q_C=x_C^2.
\]
Because distinct components of \(T-b\) are orthogonal to each other, and \(b\) is adjacent in \(C\) only to \(r_C\), we have
\[
b\cdot x=kw(b)-\sum_C c_C,
\]
and
\[
x^2=k^2w(b)-2k\sum_C c_C+\sum_C Q_C.
\]
Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
x\cdot(b-x)
&=x\cdot b-x^2\\
&=
\left(kw(b)-\sum_C c_C\right)
-
\left(k^2w(b)-2k\sum_C c_C+\sum_C Q_C\right)\\
&=
k(1-k)w(b)
+
\sum_C\left((2k-1)c_C-Q_C\right).
\end{aligned}
\]

Apply the branch inequality to each rooted component \(C\), using the same integer \(k\). It gives
\[
(2k-1)c_C-Q_C\le k(k-1)\alpha_C.
\]
Thus
\[
\begin{aligned}
x\cdot(b-x)
&\le
k(1-k)w(b)+k(k-1)\sum_C\alpha_C\\
&=
k(k-1)\left(\sum_C\alpha_C-w(b)\right).
\end{aligned}
\]
But
\[
\sum_C\alpha_C-w(b)<0.
\]

If \(k\notin\{0,1\}\), then \(k(k-1)>0\), and therefore
\[
x\cdot(b-x)<0.
\]
So no reducibility witness can have \(k\notin\{0,1\}\).

Now suppose \(k=0\) or \(k=1\). Then the upper bound above is \(0\), so
\[
x\cdot(b-x)\le0.
\]
If \(x\cdot(b-x)\ge0\), then equality must hold. Since each individual branch term satisfies
\[
(2k-1)c_C-Q_C\le0,
\]
their sum can be \(0\) only if equality holds in every branch.

But \(T\) has no weight-one vertex, so no component \(C\) contains a weight-one vertex. By the \(k=0,1\) equality analysis in the rooted branch lemma, equality in a branch forces
\[
x_C=0.
\]
Thus every \(x_C=0\). Hence
\[
x=0
\quad\text{if }k=0,
\]
and
\[
x=b
\quad\text{if }k=1.
\]
Both possibilities are excluded by \(x\ne0,b\). Therefore every \(x\ne0,b\) satisfies
\[
x\cdot(b-x)<0.
\]
So \(b\) cannot be written as a sum of two nonzero lattice elements with nonnegative pairing. Thus \(b\) is irreducible.

Combining this with the weight-one case, \(T\) always contains an irreducible vertex. \(\square\)

solution:
## Original Problem

Let $T$ denote a finite, weighted tree with vertex set $V$, edge set $E$, and weight function $w : V \longrightarrow \bZ$.
Let $L(T)$ denote the free abelian group generated by $V$, equipped with the symmetric, bilinear form $\cdot$ whose value on a pair of generators $u,v \in V$ is given by 
\[
u \cdot v = 
\begin{cases}
    w(u), & u=v; \\
    -1, & (u,v) \in E; \textup{ and} \\
    0, & u \ne v, (u,v) \notin E.
\end{cases}
\]
Suppose that the form is positive definite, and suppose that there exists a single vertex $v$ for which the vertex weight $w(v)$ is less than the vertex degree $d(v)$.

Prove that $T$ contains a vertex $u$ such that $u$ is an irreducible element of $L(T)$, i.e.~there do not exist nonzero lattice elements $a,b \in L(T)$ such that $u = a+b$ and $a \cdot b \ge 0$.

## Problem Solved

We prove the following fully self-contained statement.

Let \(T=(V,E)\) be a finite tree, let \(w:V\to\mathbb Z\) be an integer weight function, and let \(d_T(v)\) denote the degree of a vertex \(v\) in \(T\). Let
\[
L(T)=\bigoplus_{v\in V}\mathbb Z v
\]
be the free abelian group generated by the vertices of \(T\), equipped with the symmetric bilinear form determined by
\[
u\cdot u=w(u),\qquad
u\cdot v=-1\text{ if }u\ne v\text{ are adjacent},\qquad
u\cdot v=0\text{ otherwise}.
\]
Assume this form is positive definite. Assume there is exactly one vertex \(b\in V\) such that
\[
w(b)<d_T(b).
\]
Call an element \(y\in L(T)\) irreducible if there do not exist nonzero \(a,c\in L(T)\) with
\[
y=a+c
\quad\text{and}\quad
a\cdot c\ge 0.
\]
Then \(T\) contains an irreducible vertex. More precisely, every weight-one vertex is irreducible; if \(T\) has no weight-one vertex, then the unique vertex \(b\) with \(w(b)<d_T(b)\) is irreducible.

This is exactly the original problem with “a single vertex” understood in its literal uniqueness sense, so it is not a relaxation.

## Proof

For \(x\in L(T)\), write \(x^2=x\cdot x\).

### 1. Positive integral norms and weight-one vertices

If
\[
x=\sum_{v\in V} n_v v,\qquad n_v\in\mathbb Z,
\]
then
\[
x^2=\sum_{v\in V} n_v^2 w(v)-2\sum_{\{u,v\}\in E} n_u n_v.
\]
Thus \(x^2\in\mathbb Z\). Since the form is positive definite, every nonzero \(x\in L(T)\) satisfies
\[
x^2\in\mathbb Z_{>0}.
\]

Now suppose \(u\in V\) has \(w(u)=1\). If
\[
u=a+c,
\qquad a,c\ne 0,
\qquad a\cdot c\ge 0,
\]
then \(a^2,c^2\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\), so
\[
1=u^2=(a+c)^2=a^2+c^2+2a\cdot c\ge 1+1=2,
\]
a contradiction. Therefore every weight-one vertex is irreducible.

So from now on it remains only to prove that if there are no weight-one vertices, then the unique bad vertex \(b\) is irreducible.

### 2. A Schur-complement fact

We will repeatedly use the following elementary fact. Let
\[
M=
\begin{pmatrix}
a & c^T\\
c & B
\end{pmatrix},
\]
where \(B\) is symmetric positive definite. Define
\[
S=a-c^T B^{-1}c.
\]
Then
\[
M=
\begin{pmatrix}
1 & c^T B^{-1}\\
0 & I
\end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix}
S & 0\\
0 & B
\end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix}
1 & 0\\
B^{-1}c & I
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Hence \(M\) is positive definite if and only if \(S>0\). When \(S>0\),
\[
M^{-1}=
\begin{pmatrix}
S^{-1} & -S^{-1}c^TB^{-1}\\
-B^{-1}cS^{-1} & B^{-1}+B^{-1}cS^{-1}c^TB^{-1}
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

### 3. The rooted nonbad branch lemma

Let \(C\) be a finite weighted tree rooted at a vertex \(r\). Let \(d_C(v)\) denote degree inside \(C\). Let \(A\) be the Gram matrix of \(C\), written in the vertex basis, and assume \(A\) is positive definite.

Assume the rooted nonbad inequalities
\[
w(r)\ge d_C(r)+1,
\qquad
w(u)\ge d_C(u)\quad\text{for every }u\ne r.
\]
Let
\[
G=A^{-1},
\qquad
e_r=\text{the coordinate vector of }r,
\qquad
\alpha=e_r^TGe_r=(A^{-1})_{rr}.
\]

We prove:

1. \(0<\alpha\le 1\).
2. If \(\lambda\in\mathbb Z^C\) has odd root coordinate and even nonroot coordinates, meaning
   \[
   \lambda_r\equiv 1\pmod 2,
   \qquad
   \lambda_u\equiv 0\pmod 2\quad(u\ne r),
   \]
   then
   \[
   \lambda^TG\lambda\ge \alpha,
   \]
   with equality if and only if \(\lambda=\pm e_r\).
3. For every \(x\in\mathbb Z^C\), if \(c=x_r\) is the root coefficient and
   \[
   Q=x^TAx
   \]
   is its branch norm, then for every \(k\in\mathbb Z\),
   \[
   (2k-1)c-Q\le k(k-1)\alpha.
   \]
   Equality occurs only when
   \[
   x=kGe_r
   \quad\text{or}\quad
   x=(k-1)Ge_r,
   \]
   with the displayed vector integral.
4. If \(k=0\) or \(k=1\), equality with \(x\ne 0\) forces \(C\) to contain a weight-one vertex.

#### Proof of the dual-parity inequality

We prove parts 1 and 2 by induction on \(|C|\).

If \(C=\{r\}\), then \(A=[w(r)]\). Since \(w(r)\ge d_C(r)+1=1\),
\[
\alpha=\frac1{w(r)}\le 1.
\]
For an odd integer \(s\),
\[
s^2\alpha\ge \alpha,
\]
with equality exactly when \(s=\pm1\). Thus the claim holds for one vertex.

Now assume \(|C|>1\). Let \(C_1,\dots,C_m\) be the components of \(C-r\). Root \(C_i\) at the vertex \(r_i\) adjacent to \(r\). Let \(A_i\) be the Gram matrix of \(C_i\), let
\[
G_i=A_i^{-1},
\qquad
e_i=e_{r_i},
\qquad
\alpha_i=e_i^TG_ie_i.
\]
Each \(C_i\) again satisfies the rooted nonbad hypotheses: its root \(r_i\) has lost the edge to \(r\), so
\[
w(r_i)\ge d_C(r_i)=d_{C_i}(r_i)+1,
\]
and all other vertices have the same degree in \(C_i\) as in \(C\). Therefore, by induction,
\[
0<\alpha_i\le 1.
\]

Order the vertices of \(C\) by putting \(r\) first and then the vertices of \(C_1,\dots,C_m\). Then
\[
A=
\begin{pmatrix}
w(r) & -\eta^T\\
-\eta & B
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
B=A_1\oplus\cdots\oplus A_m,
\]
where the \(C_i\)-component of \(\eta\) is \(e_i\). The Schur complement is
\[
S=w(r)-\eta^TB^{-1}\eta
  =w(r)-\sum_i \alpha_i.
\]
Since \(A\) is positive definite, \(S>0\), and
\[
\alpha=(A^{-1})_{rr}=\frac1S.
\]
Also, because \(m=d_C(r)\),
\[
S=w(r)-\sum_i\alpha_i
\ge (m+1)-m
=1.
\]
Thus
\[
0<\alpha=\frac1S\le 1.
\]

Now let \(\lambda\in\mathbb Z^C\) have odd root coordinate and even nonroot coordinates. Write
\[
\lambda=(s,2\mu_1,\dots,2\mu_m),
\]
where \(s\) is odd and \(\mu_i\in\mathbb Z^{C_i}\). Define
\[
a_i=e_i^TG_i\mu_i,
\qquad
E_i=\mu_i^TG_i\mu_i,
\qquad
A_0=\sum_i a_i.
\]
Using the inverse formula from the Schur complement,
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
=
4\sum_i E_i+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}.
\]

For each \(i\), the two vectors
\[
2\mu_i-e_i
\quad\text{and}\quad
2\mu_i+e_i
\]
have odd root coordinate and even nonroot coordinates in \(C_i\). Applying the induction hypothesis to \(C_i\) gives
\[
(2\mu_i-e_i)^TG_i(2\mu_i-e_i)\ge \alpha_i,
\]
and
\[
(2\mu_i+e_i)^TG_i(2\mu_i+e_i)\ge \alpha_i.
\]
Expanding these inequalities gives
\[
4E_i-4a_i+\alpha_i\ge \alpha_i,
\qquad
4E_i+4a_i+\alpha_i\ge \alpha_i.
\]
Therefore
\[
E_i\ge |a_i|.
\]
Hence
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
\ge
4\sum_i |a_i|+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}
\ge
4|A_0|+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}.
\]

It remains to prove the scalar inequality
\[
4|A_0|+\frac{(s+2A_0)^2}{S}\ge \frac1S
\]
for \(S\ge1\) and odd \(s\). Put \(y=2A_0\). After multiplying by \(S\), the desired inequality becomes
\[
(s+y)^2+2S|y|\ge 1.
\]
If \(sy\ge0\), then
\[
|s+y|\ge |s|\ge1,
\]
so the inequality holds. If \(sy<0\), set \(a=|s|\ge1\) and \(u=|y|>0\). Then the left-hand side is
\[
(a-u)^2+2Su.
\]
If \(a=1\), this equals
\[
1+u^2+2(S-1)u>1.
\]
If \(a\ge3\), then either \(u\le a-1\), in which case \((a-u)^2\ge1\), or \(u>a-1\), in which case \(2Su>2(a-1)\ge4\). Thus the scalar inequality always holds. Equality occurs only when
\[
y=0
\quad\text{and}\quad
s=\pm1,
\]
that is, only when
\[
A_0=0
\quad\text{and}\quad
s=\pm1.
\]

Therefore
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda\ge \frac1S=\alpha.
\]
If equality holds, then the scalar equality forces \(A_0=0\) and \(s=\pm1\). In that case
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
=
4\sum_i E_i+\frac1S.
\]
Equality with \(\alpha=1/S\) forces every \(E_i=0\). Since each \(G_i\) is positive definite, \(\mu_i=0\) for every \(i\). Hence
\[
\lambda=\pm e_r.
\]
Conversely, \(\lambda=\pm e_r\) plainly gives equality. This proves the dual-parity inequality.

#### Derivation of the branch inequality

Let \(x\in\mathbb Z^C\). Write
\[
c=x_r,
\qquad
Q=x^TAx.
\]
Fix \(k\in\mathbb Z\), and set
\[
t=2k-1.
\]
Define
\[
\lambda=2Ax-te_r.
\]
Because \(A\) is integral, \(\lambda\in\mathbb Z^C\). Its root coordinate is odd, and every nonroot coordinate is even. Therefore the dual-parity inequality gives
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda\ge \alpha.
\]
Expanding,
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
=
(2Ax-te_r)^TG(2Ax-te_r)
=
4Q-4tc+t^2\alpha.
\]
Thus
\[
4Q-4tc+t^2\alpha\ge \alpha,
\]
so
\[
tc-Q\le \frac{t^2-1}{4}\alpha.
\]
Since \(t=2k-1\),
\[
\frac{t^2-1}{4}=k(k-1).
\]
Therefore
\[
(2k-1)c-Q\le k(k-1)\alpha.
\]

Equality occurs precisely when \(\lambda=\pm e_r\). If \(\lambda=e_r\), then
\[
2Ax-(2k-1)e_r=e_r,
\]
so
\[
Ax=ke_r,
\qquad
x=kGe_r.
\]
If \(\lambda=-e_r\), then
\[
2Ax-(2k-1)e_r=-e_r,
\]
so
\[
Ax=(k-1)e_r,
\qquad
x=(k-1)Ge_r.
\]
Thus equality forces one of the two stated alternatives, and conversely either integral alternative gives equality.

#### The \(k=0,1\) equality analysis

Assume \(k=0\) or \(k=1\), and suppose equality holds with \(x\ne0\). Let
\[
y=Ge_r.
\]
If \(k=1\), the equality alternatives are
\[
x=y
\quad\text{or}\quad
x=0.
\]
If \(k=0\), they are
\[
x=0
\quad\text{or}\quad
x=-y.
\]
Since \(x\ne0\), in either case \(y\in\mathbb Z^C\). Therefore its root coordinate
\[
y_r=\alpha
\]
is a positive integer. But \(0<\alpha\le1\), so
\[
\alpha=1.
\]

We now show that \(\alpha=1\) forces \(C\) to contain a weight-one vertex. Using the notation from the induction step,
\[
\alpha=\frac1S,
\qquad
S=w(r)-\sum_i\alpha_i.
\]
Thus \(\alpha=1\) gives \(S=1\). But
\[
S=w(r)-\sum_i\alpha_i
\ge w(r)-m
\ge 1.
\]
Hence equality holds throughout. If \(m=0\), then \(S=w(r)=1\), so \(r\) has weight one. If \(m>0\), then equality forces every child branch to have \(\alpha_i=1\). Repeating the same argument down a child branch and using finiteness, we eventually reach a one-vertex branch whose root has weight \(1\). Therefore \(C\) contains a weight-one vertex.

This completes the rooted nonbad branch lemma.

### 4. Deleting the unique bad vertex

Let \(b\) be the unique vertex of \(T\) satisfying
\[
w(b)<d_T(b).
\]
Let \(C\) range over the connected components of \(T-b\). For each component \(C\), let \(r_C\) be the unique vertex of \(C\) adjacent to \(b\). Let \(A_C\) be the Gram matrix of \(C\), and define
\[
\alpha_C=(A_C^{-1})_{r_Cr_C}.
\]

Because the full form on \(T\) is positive definite, each \(A_C\), being a principal submatrix, is positive definite.

Because \(b\) is the unique bad vertex, every \(u\ne b\) satisfies
\[
w(u)\ge d_T(u).
\]
For the root \(r_C\),
\[
d_C(r_C)=d_T(r_C)-1,
\]
so
\[
w(r_C)\ge d_T(r_C)=d_C(r_C)+1.
\]
For every nonroot vertex \(u\in C\),
\[
d_C(u)=d_T(u),
\]
so
\[
w(u)\ge d_C(u).
\]
Thus every component \(C\), rooted at \(r_C\), satisfies the rooted nonbad hypotheses of the branch lemma.

Now order the basis of \(L(T)\) by putting \(b\) first, followed by the vertices of the components \(C\). The full Gram matrix has block form
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
w(b) & -\eta^T\\
-\eta & B
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
B=\bigoplus_C A_C,
\]
where the \(C\)-component of \(\eta\) is the coordinate vector \(e_{r_C}\). Its Schur complement at \(b\) is
\[
w(b)-\eta^TB^{-1}\eta
=
w(b)-\sum_C \alpha_C.
\]
Since the full form is positive definite, this Schur complement is positive. Hence
\[
\sum_C \alpha_C<w(b).
\]

### 5. Final calculation: irreducibility of \(b\) when there is no weight-one vertex

Assume now that \(T\) has no weight-one vertex. We prove that \(b\) is irreducible.

It is enough to prove that for every \(x\in L(T)\) with
\[
x\ne0
\quad\text{and}\quad
x\ne b,
\]
one has
\[
x\cdot(b-x)<0.
\]
Indeed, if \(b=a+c\) with \(a,c\ne0\), then taking \(x=a\) gives \(c=b-x\).

Write \(x\) uniquely as
\[
x=kb+\sum_C x_C,
\]
where \(k\in\mathbb Z\) is the coefficient of \(b\), and \(x_C\in L(C)\). For each component \(C\), let
\[
c_C=\text{the coefficient of }r_C\text{ in }x_C,
\qquad
Q_C=x_C^2.
\]
Because distinct components of \(T-b\) are orthogonal to each other, and \(b\) is adjacent in \(C\) only to \(r_C\), we have
\[
b\cdot x=kw(b)-\sum_C c_C,
\]
and
\[
x^2=k^2w(b)-2k\sum_C c_C+\sum_C Q_C.
\]
Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
x\cdot(b-x)
&=x\cdot b-x^2\\
&=
\left(kw(b)-\sum_C c_C\right)
-
\left(k^2w(b)-2k\sum_C c_C+\sum_C Q_C\right)\\
&=
k(1-k)w(b)
+
\sum_C\left((2k-1)c_C-Q_C\right).
\end{aligned}
\]

Apply the branch inequality to each rooted component \(C\), using the same integer \(k\). It gives
\[
(2k-1)c_C-Q_C\le k(k-1)\alpha_C.
\]
Thus
\[
\begin{aligned}
x\cdot(b-x)
&\le
k(1-k)w(b)+k(k-1)\sum_C\alpha_C\\
&=
k(k-1)\left(\sum_C\alpha_C-w(b)\right).
\end{aligned}
\]
But
\[
\sum_C\alpha_C-w(b)<0.
\]

If \(k\notin\{0,1\}\), then \(k(k-1)>0\), and therefore
\[
x\cdot(b-x)<0.
\]
So no reducibility witness can have \(k\notin\{0,1\}\).

Now suppose \(k=0\) or \(k=1\). Then the upper bound above is \(0\), so
\[
x\cdot(b-x)\le0.
\]
If \(x\cdot(b-x)\ge0\), then equality must hold. Since each individual branch term satisfies
\[
(2k-1)c_C-Q_C\le0,
\]
their sum can be \(0\) only if equality holds in every branch.

But \(T\) has no weight-one vertex, so no component \(C\) contains a weight-one vertex. By the \(k=0,1\) equality analysis in the rooted branch lemma, equality in a branch forces
\[
x_C=0.
\]
Thus every \(x_C=0\). Hence
\[
x=0
\quad\text{if }k=0,
\]
and
\[
x=b
\quad\text{if }k=1.
\]
Both possibilities are excluded by \(x\ne0,b\). Therefore every \(x\ne0,b\) satisfies
\[
x\cdot(b-x)<0.
\]
So \(b\) cannot be written as a sum of two nonzero lattice elements with nonnegative pairing. Thus \(b\) is irreducible.

Combining this with the weight-one case, \(T\) always contains an irreducible vertex. \(\square\)
self_declared_problem_solved: For every finite weighted tree T=(V,E) with integer weight function w:V->Z and lattice L(T) freely generated by V with bilinear form u·u=w(u), u·v=-1 for adjacent distinct vertices, and u·v=0 for nonadjacent distinct vertices, if this form is positive definite and there is exactly one vertex b with w(b)<d_T(b), then some vertex u in V is irreducible in L(T), meaning u cannot be written as u=a+c with nonzero a,c in L(T) and a·c>=0. More precisely, every weight-one vertex is irreducible; if no weight-one vertex exists, b is irreducible. This is the original problem with 'a single vertex' interpreted as uniqueness.
advisor_statement: Let T be a finite weighted tree with positive-definite lattice form. Suppose there is a unique vertex b, equivalently a single vertex, such that w(b) < d_T(b). Then T contains a vertex u that is irreducible in L(T).
writeup_declared_problem_solved: For every finite weighted tree T=(V,E) with integer weight function w:V->Z and lattice L(T) freely generated by V with bilinear form u·u=w(u), u·v=-1 for adjacent distinct vertices, and u·v=0 for nonadjacent distinct vertices, if this form is positive definite and there is exactly one vertex b with w(b)<d_T(b), then some vertex u in V is irreducible in L(T), meaning u cannot be written as u=a+c with nonzero a,c in L(T) and a·c>=0. More precisely, every weight-one vertex is irreducible; if no weight-one vertex exists, b is irreducible. This is the original problem with 'a single vertex' interpreted as uniqueness.

description: Probe the weaker multi-bad interpretation and high-odd parity witnesses
full_text:
## Short answer

I did **not** obtain an explicit all-reducible counterexample. What I can prove is a fairly sharp obstruction:

> Any all-reducible positive-definite weighted tree, if it exists, must contain a vertex \(u\) such that every component of \(T-u\) has inverse-root entry \(<1\), and every reducing parity witness for \(u\) has coefficient \(|s|\ge 5\) at \(u\).  
> Moreover, the witness must contain a genuinely **high-odd resonant branch**. For the first possible case \(s=5\), one branch must have a vector \(X\) with root coefficient \(2\), norm \(5\), and inverse-root entry
> \[
> \frac45\le \alpha<\frac56.
> \]
> Nonbad branches from the exact-one-bad proof cannot supply such a branch.

So the multiple-bad question reduces to ruling out, or constructing, these narrow high-odd resonance branches.

---

## 1. Parity-coset criterion

Let \(u\) be a vertex. Then \(u\) is reducible iff there exists
\[
y\in u+2L(T),\qquad y\ne \pm u,\qquad y^2\le u^2.
\]

Indeed, if \(u=a+b\) with \(a,b\ne0\) and \(a\cdot b\ge0\), set \(y=a-b=2a-u\in u+2L(T)\). Then
\[
y^2=(a-b)^2=(a+b)^2-4a\cdot b=u^2-4a\cdot b\le u^2.
\]
Conversely, if such \(y\) exists, then
\[
a=\frac{u+y}{2},\qquad b=\frac{u-y}{2}
\]
are nonzero lattice vectors, \(u=a+b\), and
\[
a\cdot b=\frac{u^2-y^2}{4}\ge0.
\]

Also, any weight-one vertex is automatically irreducible, since a norm-one vector cannot split into two nonzero positive-norm vectors with nonnegative pairing. Thus an all-reducible counterexample has all weights \(\ge2\).

---

## 2. Decomposition of a witness at a vertex

Fix a vertex \(u\), put \(m=w(u)=u^2\), and write a parity witness as
\[
y=s u+2X,\qquad s\ \text{odd}.
\]
Replacing \(y\) by \(-y\) if necessary, assume \(s>0\). Let the components of \(T-u\) be \(C\). For each component, let \(r_C\) be the root adjacent to \(u\), write \(X_C\in L(C)\), and define
\[
c_C=(X_C)_{r_C},\qquad Q_C=X_C^2.
\]
Then
\[
y^2=m s^2+4\sum_C Q_C-4s\sum_C c_C.
\]
Set
\[
K=\frac{s^2-1}{4}.
\]
The condition \(y^2\le m\) is exactly
\[
\sum_C (s c_C-Q_C)\ge mK. \tag{1}
\]

For each component define the inverse-root entry
\[
\alpha_C=(A_C^{-1})_{r_Cr_C}.
\]
Positive definiteness of \(T\) gives the Schur complement inequality
\[
\Gamma_u:=\sum_C\alpha_C<m. \tag{2}
\]
Also Cauchy gives
\[
c_C^2\le \alpha_C Q_C. \tag{3}
\]
Consequently,
\[
s c_C-Q_C\le \frac{s^2}{4}\alpha_C,
\]
and summing over \(C\) gives the necessary real bound
\[
\Gamma_u\ge m\left(1-\frac1{s^2}\right). \tag{4}
\]
Thus high-odd witnesses can occur only at vertices whose Schur complement \(m-\Gamma_u\) is very small.

---

## 3. There is always an \(\alpha\)-small vertex

For an oriented edge \(u\to v\), let \(\alpha_{v|u}\) be the inverse-root entry of the component containing \(v\) after deleting the edge \(uv\), rooted at \(v\). If deleting \(uv\) gives two rooted components with inverse-root entries \(\alpha_{u|v}\) and \(\alpha_{v|u}\), then positive definiteness gives
\[
\alpha_{u|v}\alpha_{v|u}<1. \tag{5}
\]

Therefore, in any finite positive-definite tree, there is some vertex \(u\) such that every component of \(T-u\) has
\[
\alpha_C<1.
\]
Indeed, if every vertex had some adjacent component with \(\alpha\ge1\), orient each vertex toward one such component. A finite directed tree with outdegree at least one has a directed \(2\)-cycle, forcing both inverse-root entries across one edge to be \(\ge1\), contradicting (5).

Call such a vertex **\(\alpha\)-small**.

---

## 4. Low-odd witnesses force an \(\alpha\ge1\) direction

Let \(u\) be reducible.

### Unit witnesses: \(s=1\)

If \(s=1\), then \(K=0\), and reducibility requires
\[
\sum_C(c_C-Q_C)\ge0.
\]
If some nonzero component has \(c_C-Q_C\ge0\), then \(Q_C\le c_C\), so \(c_C>0\), and by Cauchy,
\[
c_C^2\le \alpha_C Q_C\le \alpha_C c_C.
\]
Thus \(\alpha_C\ge c_C\ge1\).

So any \(s=\pm1\) witness points toward a component with \(\alpha\ge1\).

### Cubic witnesses: \(s=3\)

Now \(K=2\). Put
\[
F_C=3c_C-Q_C.
\]
If \(\alpha_C<1\), then \(F_C\le1\). Indeed, if \(F_C\ge2=K\), then \(F_C=K\) forces \(\alpha_C\ge1\) by the integer maximum
\[
\max_{c\in\mathbb Z}(3c-c^2)=2.
\]
Moreover, if \(F_C=1\), then Cauchy gives \(\alpha_C\ge1/2\).

If all components had \(\alpha_C<1\), then every positive contribution is at most \(1\), and each such contribution costs at least \(1/2\) in \(\alpha\). Since a witness requires
\[
\sum_C F_C\ge2m,
\]
we would get
\[
\Gamma_u=\sum_C\alpha_C\ge m,
\]
contradicting \(\Gamma_u<m\).

Thus any \(s=\pm3\) witness also forces some component with \(\alpha\ge1\).

### Consequence

If every reducible vertex admitted a witness with coefficient \(|s|\le3\), then choosing an \(\alpha\ge1\) direction from every vertex would give the same directed-cycle contradiction as above. Hence:

> If an all-reducible counterexample exists, then at an \(\alpha\)-small vertex every reducing witness must have \(|s|\ge5\).

This strictly strengthens the unit-coefficient obstruction from Task t4.

---

## 5. Sharp high-odd obstruction

Let \(s=2k-1\ge5\), so
\[
K=k(k-1)=\frac{s^2-1}{4}.
\]
For a component \(C\), write
\[
F_C=s c_C-Q_C.
\]
If \(F_C=t>0\), then \(c_C>0\), \(Q_C=sc_C-t\), and Cauchy gives
\[
\alpha_C\ge \rho_s(t):=
\min_{\substack{c\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\\ sc>t}}
\frac{c^2}{sc-t}. \tag{6}
\]

Now suppose \(u\) is \(\alpha\)-small and has a reducing witness with coefficient \(s\). Since \(\Gamma_u<m\), if every component satisfied
\[
F_C\le K\alpha_C,
\]
then summing would give
\[
\sum_C F_C\le K\Gamma_u<Km,
\]
contradicting (1). Therefore some component must satisfy
\[
F_C>K\alpha_C. \tag{7}
\]

For such a component, \(t=F_C\) must be a **resonant integer**. Indeed, if \(\rho_s(t)\ge t/K\), then
\[
\alpha_C\ge \rho_s(t)\ge \frac{t}{K},
\]
contradicting \(t=F_C>K\alpha_C\). The inequality \(\rho_s(t)<t/K\) is equivalent to the existence of an integer \(c>0\) such that
\[
(k-1)c<t<kc. \tag{8}
\]
So a high-odd counterexample must contain a branch contribution lying strictly between two consecutive multiples \((k-1)c\) and \(kc\).

### First possible case: \(s=5\)

Here \(k=3\), \(K=6\). The only resonant integer \(t<K\) is
\[
t=5,
\]
with \(c=2\). Therefore an \(\alpha\)-small \(s=5\) witness must contain a component with
\[
F_C=5,\qquad c_C=2,\qquad Q_C=5,
\]
and
\[
\frac45\le \alpha_C<\frac56. \tag{9}
\]
The lower bound is Cauchy:
\[
\alpha_C\ge\frac{c_C^2}{Q_C}=\frac45,
\]
and the upper bound is exactly \(F_C>K\alpha_C\), i.e.
\[
5>6\alpha_C.
\]

This is a very narrow target. Nonbad rooted branches from the exact-one-bad proof satisfy the stronger inequality
\[
F_C\le K\alpha_C,
\]
so they cannot supply such a component. Thus any all-reducible counterexample must use a genuinely bad rooted component realizing this high-odd resonance.

---

## 6. Minimality of the witness does not by itself kill high-odd cases

Let
\[
P=\sum_C c_C.
\]
If \(y=s u+2X\) is locally shortest among vectors obtained by replacing \(s\) by \(s\pm2\), then comparing norms gives
\[
\frac{m(s-1)}2\le P\le \frac{m(s+1)}2. \tag{10}
\]
For \(s=5\), this is
\[
2m\le P\le3m.
\]
The resonant atom above has \(F=5\) and \(c=2\), so its ratio \(c/F=2/5\). A formal collection with total contribution \(6m\) would have \(P=(2/5)6m=12m/5\), which lies inside \([2m,3m]\). Thus minimality alone does **not** force \(s=\pm1\) or \(\pm3\); one really has to rule out or realize the resonant branches.

---

## Current conclusion

I cannot certify an all-reducible counterexample from the available examples. The strongest rigorous reduction I get is:

> Any all-reducible positive-definite weighted tree with at least one bad vertex must have an \(\alpha\)-small vertex whose reducing witnesses all have \(|s|\ge5\). For the first possible high-odd case \(s=5\), a branch must contain an integral vector with root coefficient \(2\), norm \(5\), and inverse-root entry in the narrow interval \([4/5,5/6)\).

So the remaining problem is precise: either prove that no rooted positive-definite weighted tree can realize these resonant windows, or construct enough such branches to build and verify a global counterexample.

solution:
## Short answer

I did **not** obtain an explicit all-reducible counterexample. What I can prove is a fairly sharp obstruction:

> Any all-reducible positive-definite weighted tree, if it exists, must contain a vertex \(u\) such that every component of \(T-u\) has inverse-root entry \(<1\), and every reducing parity witness for \(u\) has coefficient \(|s|\ge 5\) at \(u\).  
> Moreover, the witness must contain a genuinely **high-odd resonant branch**. For the first possible case \(s=5\), one branch must have a vector \(X\) with root coefficient \(2\), norm \(5\), and inverse-root entry
> \[
> \frac45\le \alpha<\frac56.
> \]
> Nonbad branches from the exact-one-bad proof cannot supply such a branch.

So the multiple-bad question reduces to ruling out, or constructing, these narrow high-odd resonance branches.

---

## 1. Parity-coset criterion

Let \(u\) be a vertex. Then \(u\) is reducible iff there exists
\[
y\in u+2L(T),\qquad y\ne \pm u,\qquad y^2\le u^2.
\]

Indeed, if \(u=a+b\) with \(a,b\ne0\) and \(a\cdot b\ge0\), set \(y=a-b=2a-u\in u+2L(T)\). Then
\[
y^2=(a-b)^2=(a+b)^2-4a\cdot b=u^2-4a\cdot b\le u^2.
\]
Conversely, if such \(y\) exists, then
\[
a=\frac{u+y}{2},\qquad b=\frac{u-y}{2}
\]
are nonzero lattice vectors, \(u=a+b\), and
\[
a\cdot b=\frac{u^2-y^2}{4}\ge0.
\]

Also, any weight-one vertex is automatically irreducible, since a norm-one vector cannot split into two nonzero positive-norm vectors with nonnegative pairing. Thus an all-reducible counterexample has all weights \(\ge2\).

---

## 2. Decomposition of a witness at a vertex

Fix a vertex \(u\), put \(m=w(u)=u^2\), and write a parity witness as
\[
y=s u+2X,\qquad s\ \text{odd}.
\]
Replacing \(y\) by \(-y\) if necessary, assume \(s>0\). Let the components of \(T-u\) be \(C\). For each component, let \(r_C\) be the root adjacent to \(u\), write \(X_C\in L(C)\), and define
\[
c_C=(X_C)_{r_C},\qquad Q_C=X_C^2.
\]
Then
\[
y^2=m s^2+4\sum_C Q_C-4s\sum_C c_C.
\]
Set
\[
K=\frac{s^2-1}{4}.
\]
The condition \(y^2\le m\) is exactly
\[
\sum_C (s c_C-Q_C)\ge mK. \tag{1}
\]

For each component define the inverse-root entry
\[
\alpha_C=(A_C^{-1})_{r_Cr_C}.
\]
Positive definiteness of \(T\) gives the Schur complement inequality
\[
\Gamma_u:=\sum_C\alpha_C<m. \tag{2}
\]
Also Cauchy gives
\[
c_C^2\le \alpha_C Q_C. \tag{3}
\]
Consequently,
\[
s c_C-Q_C\le \frac{s^2}{4}\alpha_C,
\]
and summing over \(C\) gives the necessary real bound
\[
\Gamma_u\ge m\left(1-\frac1{s^2}\right). \tag{4}
\]
Thus high-odd witnesses can occur only at vertices whose Schur complement \(m-\Gamma_u\) is very small.

---

## 3. There is always an \(\alpha\)-small vertex

For an oriented edge \(u\to v\), let \(\alpha_{v|u}\) be the inverse-root entry of the component containing \(v\) after deleting the edge \(uv\), rooted at \(v\). If deleting \(uv\) gives two rooted components with inverse-root entries \(\alpha_{u|v}\) and \(\alpha_{v|u}\), then positive definiteness gives
\[
\alpha_{u|v}\alpha_{v|u}<1. \tag{5}
\]

Therefore, in any finite positive-definite tree, there is some vertex \(u\) such that every component of \(T-u\) has
\[
\alpha_C<1.
\]
Indeed, if every vertex had some adjacent component with \(\alpha\ge1\), orient each vertex toward one such component. A finite directed tree with outdegree at least one has a directed \(2\)-cycle, forcing both inverse-root entries across one edge to be \(\ge1\), contradicting (5).

Call such a vertex **\(\alpha\)-small**.

---

## 4. Low-odd witnesses force an \(\alpha\ge1\) direction

Let \(u\) be reducible.

### Unit witnesses: \(s=1\)

If \(s=1\), then \(K=0\), and reducibility requires
\[
\sum_C(c_C-Q_C)\ge0.
\]
If some nonzero component has \(c_C-Q_C\ge0\), then \(Q_C\le c_C\), so \(c_C>0\), and by Cauchy,
\[
c_C^2\le \alpha_C Q_C\le \alpha_C c_C.
\]
Thus \(\alpha_C\ge c_C\ge1\).

So any \(s=\pm1\) witness points toward a component with \(\alpha\ge1\).

### Cubic witnesses: \(s=3\)

Now \(K=2\). Put
\[
F_C=3c_C-Q_C.
\]
If \(\alpha_C<1\), then \(F_C\le1\). Indeed, if \(F_C\ge2=K\), then \(F_C=K\) forces \(\alpha_C\ge1\) by the integer maximum
\[
\max_{c\in\mathbb Z}(3c-c^2)=2.
\]
Moreover, if \(F_C=1\), then Cauchy gives \(\alpha_C\ge1/2\).

If all components had \(\alpha_C<1\), then every positive contribution is at most \(1\), and each such contribution costs at least \(1/2\) in \(\alpha\). Since a witness requires
\[
\sum_C F_C\ge2m,
\]
we would get
\[
\Gamma_u=\sum_C\alpha_C\ge m,
\]
contradicting \(\Gamma_u<m\).

Thus any \(s=\pm3\) witness also forces some component with \(\alpha\ge1\).

### Consequence

If every reducible vertex admitted a witness with coefficient \(|s|\le3\), then choosing an \(\alpha\ge1\) direction from every vertex would give the same directed-cycle contradiction as above. Hence:

> If an all-reducible counterexample exists, then at an \(\alpha\)-small vertex every reducing witness must have \(|s|\ge5\).

This strictly strengthens the unit-coefficient obstruction from Task t4.

---

## 5. Sharp high-odd obstruction

Let \(s=2k-1\ge5\), so
\[
K=k(k-1)=\frac{s^2-1}{4}.
\]
For a component \(C\), write
\[
F_C=s c_C-Q_C.
\]
If \(F_C=t>0\), then \(c_C>0\), \(Q_C=sc_C-t\), and Cauchy gives
\[
\alpha_C\ge \rho_s(t):=
\min_{\substack{c\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\\ sc>t}}
\frac{c^2}{sc-t}. \tag{6}
\]

Now suppose \(u\) is \(\alpha\)-small and has a reducing witness with coefficient \(s\). Since \(\Gamma_u<m\), if every component satisfied
\[
F_C\le K\alpha_C,
\]
then summing would give
\[
\sum_C F_C\le K\Gamma_u<Km,
\]
contradicting (1). Therefore some component must satisfy
\[
F_C>K\alpha_C. \tag{7}
\]

For such a component, \(t=F_C\) must be a **resonant integer**. Indeed, if \(\rho_s(t)\ge t/K\), then
\[
\alpha_C\ge \rho_s(t)\ge \frac{t}{K},
\]
contradicting \(t=F_C>K\alpha_C\). The inequality \(\rho_s(t)<t/K\) is equivalent to the existence of an integer \(c>0\) such that
\[
(k-1)c<t<kc. \tag{8}
\]
So a high-odd counterexample must contain a branch contribution lying strictly between two consecutive multiples \((k-1)c\) and \(kc\).

### First possible case: \(s=5\)

Here \(k=3\), \(K=6\). The only resonant integer \(t<K\) is
\[
t=5,
\]
with \(c=2\). Therefore an \(\alpha\)-small \(s=5\) witness must contain a component with
\[
F_C=5,\qquad c_C=2,\qquad Q_C=5,
\]
and
\[
\frac45\le \alpha_C<\frac56. \tag{9}
\]
The lower bound is Cauchy:
\[
\alpha_C\ge\frac{c_C^2}{Q_C}=\frac45,
\]
and the upper bound is exactly \(F_C>K\alpha_C\), i.e.
\[
5>6\alpha_C.
\]

This is a very narrow target. Nonbad rooted branches from the exact-one-bad proof satisfy the stronger inequality
\[
F_C\le K\alpha_C,
\]
so they cannot supply such a component. Thus any all-reducible counterexample must use a genuinely bad rooted component realizing this high-odd resonance.

---

## 6. Minimality of the witness does not by itself kill high-odd cases

Let
\[
P=\sum_C c_C.
\]
If \(y=s u+2X\) is locally shortest among vectors obtained by replacing \(s\) by \(s\pm2\), then comparing norms gives
\[
\frac{m(s-1)}2\le P\le \frac{m(s+1)}2. \tag{10}
\]
For \(s=5\), this is
\[
2m\le P\le3m.
\]
The resonant atom above has \(F=5\) and \(c=2\), so its ratio \(c/F=2/5\). A formal collection with total contribution \(6m\) would have \(P=(2/5)6m=12m/5\), which lies inside \([2m,3m]\). Thus minimality alone does **not** force \(s=\pm1\) or \(\pm3\); one really has to rule out or realize the resonant branches.

---

## Current conclusion

I cannot certify an all-reducible counterexample from the available examples. The strongest rigorous reduction I get is:

> Any all-reducible positive-definite weighted tree with at least one bad vertex must have an \(\alpha\)-small vertex whose reducing witnesses all have \(|s|\ge5\). For the first possible high-odd case \(s=5\), a branch must contain an integral vector with root coefficient \(2\), norm \(5\), and inverse-root entry in the narrow interval \([4/5,5/6)\).

So the remaining problem is precise: either prove that no rooted positive-definite weighted tree can realize these resonant windows, or construct enough such branches to build and verify a global counterexample.

Let $L(T)$ denote the free abelian group generated by $V$, equipped with the symmetric, bilinear form $\cdot$ whose value on a pair of generators $u,v \in V$ is given by 
\[
u \cdot v = 
\begin{cases}
    w(u), & u=v; \\
    -1, & (u,v) \in E; \textup{ and} \\
    0, & u \ne v, (u,v) \notin E.
\end{cases}
\]
Suppose that the form is positive definite, and suppose that there exists a single vertex $v$ for which the vertex weight $w(v)$ is less than the vertex degree $d(v)$.

Prove that $T$ contains a vertex $u$ such that $u$ is an irreducible element of $L(T)$, i.e.~there do not exist nonzero lattice elements $a,b \in L(T)$ such that $u = a+b$ and $a \cdot b \ge 0$.

description: writeup (full problem): Let T be a finite weighted tree with positive-definite lattice form. Suppose there is a unique vertex b such that w(b)<d_T(b). Then T contains a vertex that is irreducible in L(T); indeed, if T has a weight-one vertex then such a vertex is irreducible, and otherwise the unique bad vertex b is irreducible.
full_text:
## Original Problem

Let $T$ denote a finite, weighted tree with vertex set $V$, edge set $E$, and weight function $w : V \longrightarrow \bZ$.
Let $L(T)$ denote the free abelian group generated by $V$, equipped with the symmetric, bilinear form $\cdot$ whose value on a pair of generators $u,v \in V$ is given by 
\[
u \cdot v = 
\begin{cases}
    w(u), & u=v; \\
    -1, & (u,v) \in E; \textup{ and} \\
    0, & u \ne v, (u,v) \notin E.
\end{cases}
\]
Suppose that the form is positive definite, and suppose that there exists a single vertex $v$ for which the vertex weight $w(v)$ is less than the vertex degree $d(v)$.

Prove that $T$ contains a vertex $u$ such that $u$ is an irreducible element of $L(T)$, i.e.~there do not exist nonzero lattice elements $a,b \in L(T)$ such that $u = a+b$ and $a \cdot b \ge 0$.

## Problem Solved

Let \(T=(V,E,w)\) be a finite weighted tree, meaning that \((V,E)\) is a finite connected acyclic graph and \(w:V\to\mathbb Z\) is an integer weight function. For \(v\in V\), let \(d_T(v)\) be its graph degree. Let
\[
L(T)=\bigoplus_{v\in V}\mathbb Z v
\]
with symmetric bilinear form determined by \(v\cdot v=w(v)\), by \(u\cdot v=-1\) when \(u\neq v\) are adjacent, and by \(u\cdot v=0\) when \(u\neq v\) are not adjacent. Assume this form is positive definite. Call a vertex \(v\) bad if \(w(v)<d_T(v)\). Call an element \(y\in L(T)\) irreducible if there are no nonzero \(p,q\in L(T)\) such that \(y=p+q\) and \(p\cdot q\ge0\).

The proof establishes: if \(T\) has exactly one bad vertex, then \(T\) contains an irreducible vertex. More precisely, every weight-one vertex is irreducible; if \(T\) has no weight-one vertex, then the unique bad vertex is irreducible. This is the original problem exactly, with “a single vertex” read as “exactly one vertex.”

## Proof

For \(x=\sum_{v\in V} n_v v\in L(T)\),
\[
x^2=x\cdot x=\sum_{v\in V}n_v^2w(v)-2\sum_{\{u,v\}\in E}n_un_v.
\]
Thus \(x^2\in\mathbb Z\). Since the form is positive definite, every nonzero \(x\in L(T)\) has \(x^2\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\).

### 1. Weight-one vertices are irreducible

Suppose \(u\in V\) satisfies \(w(u)=1\). If
\[
u=p+q,\qquad p,q\neq0,\qquad p\cdot q\ge0,
\]
then \(p^2,q^2\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\). Hence
\[
1=u^2=(p+q)^2=p^2+q^2+2p\cdot q\ge1+1=2,
\]
a contradiction. Therefore every weight-one vertex is irreducible.

So, from now on, assume \(T\) has no weight-one vertex. Since each vertex has positive integral norm, this means all vertex weights are at least \(2\). Let \(b\) be the unique bad vertex.

### 2. Delete the unique bad vertex

Let \(\mathcal C\) be the set of connected components of \(T-\{b\}\). For each component \(C\in\mathcal C\), let \(r_C\) be the unique vertex of \(C\) adjacent to \(b\). Let \(A_C\) be the Gram matrix of the form restricted to \(L(C)\), and set
\[
\alpha_C=(A_C^{-1})_{r_Cr_C}.
\]

Because the full form on \(T\) is positive definite, every principal submatrix \(A_C\) is positive definite. Also, because \(b\) is the unique bad vertex, every \(u\neq b\) satisfies
\[
w(u)\ge d_T(u).
\]
For the root \(r_C\),
\[
d_C(r_C)=d_T(r_C)-1,
\]
so
\[
w(r_C)\ge d_T(r_C)=d_C(r_C)+1.
\]
For every nonroot vertex \(u\in C\),
\[
d_C(u)=d_T(u),
\]
so
\[
w(u)\ge d_C(u).
\]
Thus every component \(C\), rooted at \(r_C\), is a rooted nonbad branch in the sense used below.

### 3. A Schur complement fact

We use the following elementary fact. Let
\[
M=\begin{pmatrix}
\theta & \gamma^T\\
\gamma & B
\end{pmatrix},
\]
where \(B\) is symmetric positive definite. Define
\[
\sigma=\theta-\gamma^T B^{-1}\gamma.
\]
Then, for real \(t\) and real column vector \(z\),
\[
\begin{pmatrix}t\\ z\end{pmatrix}^T
M
\begin{pmatrix}t\\ z\end{pmatrix}
=
(z+B^{-1}\gamma t)^TB(z+B^{-1}\gamma t)+\sigma t^2.
\]
Therefore \(M\) is positive definite if and only if \(\sigma>0\). When \(\sigma>0\),
\[
M^{-1}=
\begin{pmatrix}
\sigma^{-1} & -\sigma^{-1}\gamma^TB^{-1}\\
-B^{-1}\gamma\sigma^{-1} & B^{-1}+B^{-1}\gamma\sigma^{-1}\gamma^TB^{-1}
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

### 4. Rooted branch lemma

Let \(C\) be a finite weighted tree rooted at \(r\). Let \(d_C\) denote degree inside \(C\). Let \(A\) be the Gram matrix of \(C\), assume \(A\) is positive definite, and assume
\[
w(r)\ge d_C(r)+1,\qquad w(u)\ge d_C(u)\quad(u\neq r).
\]
Let \(G=A^{-1}\), let \(e_r\) be the standard coordinate vector of the root, and set
\[
\alpha=e_r^TGe_r=(A^{-1})_{rr}.
\]

We prove:

1. \(0<\alpha\le1\).
2. If \(\lambda\in\mathbb Z^C\) has odd root coordinate and even nonroot coordinates, then
   \[
   \lambda^TG\lambda\ge\alpha,
   \]
   with equality if and only if \(\lambda=\pm e_r\).
3. For every \(x\in\mathbb Z^C\), writing \(c=x_r\) and \(Q=x^TAx\), and for every \(k\in\mathbb Z\),
   \[
   (2k-1)c-Q\le k(k-1)\alpha.
   \]
4. If \(k=0\) or \(k=1\), then nonzero equality in this inequality forces \(C\) to contain a weight-one vertex.

#### Proof of the dual parity statement

We prove parts 1 and 2 by induction on \(|C|\).

If \(C=\{r\}\), then \(A=[w(r)]\). Since \(w(r)\ge1\),
\[
\alpha=\frac1{w(r)}\le1.
\]
For an odd integer \(s\),
\[
s^2\alpha\ge\alpha,
\]
with equality if and only if \(s=\pm1\). Thus the claim holds for one vertex.

Now assume \(|C|>1\). Let \(C_1,\dots,C_m\) be the connected components of \(C-\{r\}\). Root \(C_i\) at the vertex \(r_i\) adjacent to \(r\). Let \(A_i\) be the Gram matrix of \(C_i\), let \(G_i=A_i^{-1}\), let \(e_i=e_{r_i}\), and define
\[
\alpha_i=e_i^TG_ie_i.
\]
Each \(C_i\) again satisfies the rooted nonbad hypotheses: its root \(r_i\) has lost the edge to \(r\), so
\[
w(r_i)\ge d_C(r_i)=d_{C_i}(r_i)+1,
\]
and every other vertex has the same degree in \(C_i\) as in \(C\). By induction, \(0<\alpha_i\le1\).

Order the vertices by putting \(r\) first, followed by the vertices of the \(C_i\). Then
\[
A=
\begin{pmatrix}
w(r)&-\eta^T\\
-\eta&B
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
B=A_1\oplus\cdots\oplus A_m,
\]
where the \(C_i\)-component of \(\eta\) is \(e_i\). The Schur complement is
\[
S=w(r)-\eta^TB^{-1}\eta=w(r)-\sum_i\alpha_i.
\]
Since \(A\) is positive definite, \(S>0\), and the inverse formula gives
\[
\alpha=(A^{-1})_{rr}=\frac1S.
\]
Also,
\[
S=w(r)-\sum_i\alpha_i\ge (m+1)-m=1.
\]
Hence \(0<\alpha\le1\).

Now let \(\lambda\in\mathbb Z^C\) have odd root coordinate and even nonroot coordinates. Write
\[
\lambda=(s,2\mu_1,\dots,2\mu_m),
\]
where \(s\) is odd and \(\mu_i\in\mathbb Z^{C_i}\). Define
\[
\rho_i=e_i^TG_i\mu_i,\qquad E_i=\mu_i^TG_i\mu_i,\qquad R=\sum_i\rho_i.
\]
Using the inverse formula with the block decomposition above,
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
=
4\sum_i E_i+\frac{(s+2R)^2}{S}.
\]

For each \(i\), the two vectors
\[
2\mu_i-e_i,\qquad 2\mu_i+e_i
\]
have odd coordinate at the root \(r_i\) and even coordinates elsewhere. By the induction hypothesis,
\[
(2\mu_i-e_i)^TG_i(2\mu_i-e_i)\ge\alpha_i,
\]
and
\[
(2\mu_i+e_i)^TG_i(2\mu_i+e_i)\ge\alpha_i.
\]
Expanding gives
\[
4E_i-4\rho_i+\alpha_i\ge\alpha_i,
\qquad
4E_i+4\rho_i+\alpha_i\ge\alpha_i.
\]
Therefore
\[
E_i\ge|\rho_i|.
\]
Thus
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
\ge
4\sum_i|\rho_i|+\frac{(s+2R)^2}{S}
\ge
4|R|+\frac{(s+2R)^2}{S}.
\]

It remains to prove
\[
4|R|+\frac{(s+2R)^2}{S}\ge\frac1S.
\]
Multiplying by \(S\ge1\), this is
\[
4S|R|+(s+2R)^2\ge1.
\]
Set \(y=2R\). Then this becomes
\[
2S|y|+(s+y)^2\ge1.
\]
If \(y=0\), this is \(s^2\ge1\), with equality exactly when \(s=\pm1\). If \(y\neq0\) and \(sy\ge0\), then \(|s+y|>|s|\ge1\), so the inequality is strict. If \(sy<0\), put \(h=|s|\ge1\) and \(u=|y|>0\). Then the left side is
\[
(h-u)^2+2Su.
\]
If \(h=1\), this equals
\[
1+u^2+2(S-1)u>1.
\]
If \(h\ge3\), then either \(u\le h-1\), in which case \((h-u)^2\ge1\), or \(u>h-1\), in which case \(2Su>2(h-1)\ge4\). Hence the inequality holds, and equality occurs only when \(R=0\) and \(s=\pm1\).

Therefore
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda\ge\frac1S=\alpha.
\]
If equality holds, then the scalar equality forces \(R=0\) and \(s=\pm1\). The exact formula then becomes
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda=4\sum_iE_i+\frac1S.
\]
Equality with \(\alpha=1/S\) forces every \(E_i=0\). Since each \(G_i\) is positive definite, every \(\mu_i=0\). Hence \(\lambda=\pm e_r\). Conversely, \(\lambda=\pm e_r\) plainly gives equality.

This proves the dual parity statement.

#### Derivation of the branch inequality

Let \(x\in\mathbb Z^C\), let \(c=x_r\), and let \(Q=x^TAx\). Fix \(k\in\mathbb Z\) and put
\[
t=2k-1.
\]
Define
\[
\lambda=2Ax-te_r.
\]
Because \(A\) is integral, \(\lambda\in\mathbb Z^C\). Its root coordinate is odd, and every nonroot coordinate is even. Therefore the dual parity statement gives
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda\ge\alpha.
\]
Expanding,
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
=
(2Ax-te_r)^TG(2Ax-te_r)
=
4Q-4tc+t^2\alpha.
\]
Hence
\[
4Q-4tc+t^2\alpha\ge\alpha,
\]
so
\[
tc-Q\le\frac{t^2-1}{4}\alpha.
\]
Since \(t=2k-1\),
\[
\frac{t^2-1}{4}=k(k-1).
\]
Therefore
\[
(2k-1)c-Q\le k(k-1)\alpha.
\]

Equality holds if and only if \(\lambda=\pm e_r\). If \(\lambda=e_r\), then
\[
2Ax-(2k-1)e_r=e_r,
\]
so
\[
Ax=ke_r,\qquad x=kGe_r.
\]
If \(\lambda=-e_r\), then
\[
Ax=(k-1)e_r,\qquad x=(k-1)Ge_r.
\]
Thus equality occurs exactly when \(x=kGe_r\) or \(x=(k-1)Ge_r\), with the displayed vector integral.

#### Equality for \(k=0\) and \(k=1\)

Let
\[
y=Ge_r.
\]
For \(k=1\), equality implies
\[
x=0\quad\text{or}\quad x=y.
\]
For \(k=0\), equality implies
\[
x=0\quad\text{or}\quad x=-y.
\]
Thus nonzero equality for \(k=0\) or \(k=1\) forces \(y\in\mathbb Z^C\). Since \(y_r=\alpha\) and \(0<\alpha\le1\), integrality forces \(\alpha=1\).

We now show that \(\alpha=1\) forces \(C\) to contain a weight-one vertex. If \(C=\{r\}\), then \(\alpha=1/w(r)\), so \(\alpha=1\) gives \(w(r)=1\). Otherwise, using the child-branch notation above,
\[
\alpha=\frac1S,
\qquad
S=w(r)-\sum_i\alpha_i.
\]
Thus \(\alpha=1\) gives \(S=1\). But
\[
S=w(r)-\sum_i\alpha_i\ge w(r)-m\ge1.
\]
Therefore equality holds throughout. If \(m=0\), then \(w(r)=1\). If \(m>0\), equality forces every child branch to have \(\alpha_i=1\). Descending through a child branch and repeating, finiteness eventually leads to a one-vertex branch whose root has weight \(1\). Hence \(C\) contains a weight-one vertex.

Consequently, if \(C\) contains no weight-one vertex, then equality in the branch inequality for \(k=0\) or \(k=1\) occurs only when \(x=0\).

This completes the rooted branch lemma.

### 5. The global Schur complement inequality

Return to the original tree \(T\) and its unique bad vertex \(b\). Order the vertices by putting \(b\) first and then the vertices of the components \(C\in\mathcal C\). The full Gram matrix has block form
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
w(b)&-\eta^T\\
-\eta&B
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
B=\bigoplus_{C\in\mathcal C}A_C,
\]
where the \(C\)-component of \(\eta\) is \(e_{r_C}\). The Schur complement at \(b\) is
\[
w(b)-\eta^TB^{-1}\eta
=
w(b)-\sum_{C\in\mathcal C}\alpha_C.
\]
Because the full form is positive definite, this Schur complement is positive. Therefore
\[
\sum_{C\in\mathcal C}\alpha_C<w(b).
\]

### 6. Irreducibility of the unique bad vertex when there is no weight-one vertex

We prove that \(b\) is irreducible. It is enough to show that for every \(x\in L(T)\) with \(x\neq0\) and \(x\neq b\),
\[
x\cdot(b-x)<0.
\]
Indeed, if \(b=p+q\) with \(p,q\neq0\), then taking \(x=p\) gives \(q=b-x\), so \(x\neq0,b\).

Write
\[
x=kb+\sum_{C\in\mathcal C}x_C,
\]
where \(k\in\mathbb Z\) and \(x_C\in L(C)\). Let \(c_C\) be the coefficient of \(r_C\) in \(x_C\), and let
\[
Q_C=x_C^2
\]
computed using the form on \(C\). Since \(b\) is adjacent in \(C\) only to \(r_C\),
\[
b\cdot x=kw(b)-\sum_C c_C.
\]
Also, distinct components of \(T-\{b\}\) are orthogonal, so
\[
x^2=k^2w(b)-2k\sum_Cc_C+\sum_CQ_C.
\]
Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
x\cdot(b-x)
&=x\cdot b-x^2\\
&=
\left(kw(b)-\sum_Cc_C\right)
-
\left(k^2w(b)-2k\sum_Cc_C+\sum_CQ_C\right)\\
&=
k(1-k)w(b)
+
\sum_C\bigl((2k-1)c_C-Q_C\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]

Apply the rooted branch inequality to each component \(C\), with the same integer \(k\). Since each \(C\) is a rooted nonbad branch,
\[
(2k-1)c_C-Q_C\le k(k-1)\alpha_C.
\]
Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
x\cdot(b-x)
&\le k(1-k)w(b)+k(k-1)\sum_C\alpha_C\\
&=
k(k-1)\left(\sum_C\alpha_C-w(b)\right).
\end{aligned}
\]
But
\[
\sum_C\alpha_C-w(b)<0.
\]

If \(k\notin\{0,1\}\), then \(k(k-1)>0\), so
\[
x\cdot(b-x)<0.
\]

Now suppose \(k=0\) or \(k=1\). Then the upper bound above is \(0\), and each individual branch term satisfies
\[
(2k-1)c_C-Q_C\le0.
\]
If \(x\cdot(b-x)\ge0\), then every branch inequality must be an equality. Since \(T\) has no weight-one vertex, no component \(C\) contains a weight-one vertex. By the equality statement in the rooted branch lemma, equality for \(k=0\) or \(k=1\) forces
\[
x_C=0
\]
for every component \(C\).

Thus, if \(k=0\), then \(x=0\), and if \(k=1\), then \(x=b\). Both are excluded when \(x\neq0,b\). Therefore every \(x\neq0,b\) satisfies
\[
x\cdot(b-x)<0.
\]

Consequently, there are no nonzero \(p,q\in L(T)\) with
\[
b=p+q,\qquad p\cdot q\ge0.
\]
Thus \(b\) is irreducible. Combining this with the weight-one case, \(T\) always contains an irreducible vertex. \(\square\)

solution:
## Original Problem

Let $T$ denote a finite, weighted tree with vertex set $V$, edge set $E$, and weight function $w : V \longrightarrow \bZ$.
Let $L(T)$ denote the free abelian group generated by $V$, equipped with the symmetric, bilinear form $\cdot$ whose value on a pair of generators $u,v \in V$ is given by 
\[
u \cdot v = 
\begin{cases}
    w(u), & u=v; \\
    -1, & (u,v) \in E; \textup{ and} \\
    0, & u \ne v, (u,v) \notin E.
\end{cases}
\]
Suppose that the form is positive definite, and suppose that there exists a single vertex $v$ for which the vertex weight $w(v)$ is less than the vertex degree $d(v)$.

Prove that $T$ contains a vertex $u$ such that $u$ is an irreducible element of $L(T)$, i.e.~there do not exist nonzero lattice elements $a,b \in L(T)$ such that $u = a+b$ and $a \cdot b \ge 0$.

## Problem Solved

Let \(T=(V,E,w)\) be a finite weighted tree, meaning that \((V,E)\) is a finite connected acyclic graph and \(w:V\to\mathbb Z\) is an integer weight function. For \(v\in V\), let \(d_T(v)\) be its graph degree. Let
\[
L(T)=\bigoplus_{v\in V}\mathbb Z v
\]
with symmetric bilinear form determined by \(v\cdot v=w(v)\), by \(u\cdot v=-1\) when \(u\neq v\) are adjacent, and by \(u\cdot v=0\) when \(u\neq v\) are not adjacent. Assume this form is positive definite. Call a vertex \(v\) bad if \(w(v)<d_T(v)\). Call an element \(y\in L(T)\) irreducible if there are no nonzero \(p,q\in L(T)\) such that \(y=p+q\) and \(p\cdot q\ge0\).

The proof establishes: if \(T\) has exactly one bad vertex, then \(T\) contains an irreducible vertex. More precisely, every weight-one vertex is irreducible; if \(T\) has no weight-one vertex, then the unique bad vertex is irreducible. This is the original problem exactly, with “a single vertex” read as “exactly one vertex.”

## Proof

For \(x=\sum_{v\in V} n_v v\in L(T)\),
\[
x^2=x\cdot x=\sum_{v\in V}n_v^2w(v)-2\sum_{\{u,v\}\in E}n_un_v.
\]
Thus \(x^2\in\mathbb Z\). Since the form is positive definite, every nonzero \(x\in L(T)\) has \(x^2\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\).

### 1. Weight-one vertices are irreducible

Suppose \(u\in V\) satisfies \(w(u)=1\). If
\[
u=p+q,\qquad p,q\neq0,\qquad p\cdot q\ge0,
\]
then \(p^2,q^2\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\). Hence
\[
1=u^2=(p+q)^2=p^2+q^2+2p\cdot q\ge1+1=2,
\]
a contradiction. Therefore every weight-one vertex is irreducible.

So, from now on, assume \(T\) has no weight-one vertex. Since each vertex has positive integral norm, this means all vertex weights are at least \(2\). Let \(b\) be the unique bad vertex.

### 2. Delete the unique bad vertex

Let \(\mathcal C\) be the set of connected components of \(T-\{b\}\). For each component \(C\in\mathcal C\), let \(r_C\) be the unique vertex of \(C\) adjacent to \(b\). Let \(A_C\) be the Gram matrix of the form restricted to \(L(C)\), and set
\[
\alpha_C=(A_C^{-1})_{r_Cr_C}.
\]

Because the full form on \(T\) is positive definite, every principal submatrix \(A_C\) is positive definite. Also, because \(b\) is the unique bad vertex, every \(u\neq b\) satisfies
\[
w(u)\ge d_T(u).
\]
For the root \(r_C\),
\[
d_C(r_C)=d_T(r_C)-1,
\]
so
\[
w(r_C)\ge d_T(r_C)=d_C(r_C)+1.
\]
For every nonroot vertex \(u\in C\),
\[
d_C(u)=d_T(u),
\]
so
\[
w(u)\ge d_C(u).
\]
Thus every component \(C\), rooted at \(r_C\), is a rooted nonbad branch in the sense used below.

### 3. A Schur complement fact

We use the following elementary fact. Let
\[
M=\begin{pmatrix}
\theta & \gamma^T\\
\gamma & B
\end{pmatrix},
\]
where \(B\) is symmetric positive definite. Define
\[
\sigma=\theta-\gamma^T B^{-1}\gamma.
\]
Then, for real \(t\) and real column vector \(z\),
\[
\begin{pmatrix}t\\ z\end{pmatrix}^T
M
\begin{pmatrix}t\\ z\end{pmatrix}
=
(z+B^{-1}\gamma t)^TB(z+B^{-1}\gamma t)+\sigma t^2.
\]
Therefore \(M\) is positive definite if and only if \(\sigma>0\). When \(\sigma>0\),
\[
M^{-1}=
\begin{pmatrix}
\sigma^{-1} & -\sigma^{-1}\gamma^TB^{-1}\\
-B^{-1}\gamma\sigma^{-1} & B^{-1}+B^{-1}\gamma\sigma^{-1}\gamma^TB^{-1}
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

### 4. Rooted branch lemma

Let \(C\) be a finite weighted tree rooted at \(r\). Let \(d_C\) denote degree inside \(C\). Let \(A\) be the Gram matrix of \(C\), assume \(A\) is positive definite, and assume
\[
w(r)\ge d_C(r)+1,\qquad w(u)\ge d_C(u)\quad(u\neq r).
\]
Let \(G=A^{-1}\), let \(e_r\) be the standard coordinate vector of the root, and set
\[
\alpha=e_r^TGe_r=(A^{-1})_{rr}.
\]

We prove:

1. \(0<\alpha\le1\).
2. If \(\lambda\in\mathbb Z^C\) has odd root coordinate and even nonroot coordinates, then
   \[
   \lambda^TG\lambda\ge\alpha,
   \]
   with equality if and only if \(\lambda=\pm e_r\).
3. For every \(x\in\mathbb Z^C\), writing \(c=x_r\) and \(Q=x^TAx\), and for every \(k\in\mathbb Z\),
   \[
   (2k-1)c-Q\le k(k-1)\alpha.
   \]
4. If \(k=0\) or \(k=1\), then nonzero equality in this inequality forces \(C\) to contain a weight-one vertex.

#### Proof of the dual parity statement

We prove parts 1 and 2 by induction on \(|C|\).

If \(C=\{r\}\), then \(A=[w(r)]\). Since \(w(r)\ge1\),
\[
\alpha=\frac1{w(r)}\le1.
\]
For an odd integer \(s\),
\[
s^2\alpha\ge\alpha,
\]
with equality if and only if \(s=\pm1\). Thus the claim holds for one vertex.

Now assume \(|C|>1\). Let \(C_1,\dots,C_m\) be the connected components of \(C-\{r\}\). Root \(C_i\) at the vertex \(r_i\) adjacent to \(r\). Let \(A_i\) be the Gram matrix of \(C_i\), let \(G_i=A_i^{-1}\), let \(e_i=e_{r_i}\), and define
\[
\alpha_i=e_i^TG_ie_i.
\]
Each \(C_i\) again satisfies the rooted nonbad hypotheses: its root \(r_i\) has lost the edge to \(r\), so
\[
w(r_i)\ge d_C(r_i)=d_{C_i}(r_i)+1,
\]
and every other vertex has the same degree in \(C_i\) as in \(C\). By induction, \(0<\alpha_i\le1\).

Order the vertices by putting \(r\) first, followed by the vertices of the \(C_i\). Then
\[
A=
\begin{pmatrix}
w(r)&-\eta^T\\
-\eta&B
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
B=A_1\oplus\cdots\oplus A_m,
\]
where the \(C_i\)-component of \(\eta\) is \(e_i\). The Schur complement is
\[
S=w(r)-\eta^TB^{-1}\eta=w(r)-\sum_i\alpha_i.
\]
Since \(A\) is positive definite, \(S>0\), and the inverse formula gives
\[
\alpha=(A^{-1})_{rr}=\frac1S.
\]
Also,
\[
S=w(r)-\sum_i\alpha_i\ge (m+1)-m=1.
\]
Hence \(0<\alpha\le1\).

Now let \(\lambda\in\mathbb Z^C\) have odd root coordinate and even nonroot coordinates. Write
\[
\lambda=(s,2\mu_1,\dots,2\mu_m),
\]
where \(s\) is odd and \(\mu_i\in\mathbb Z^{C_i}\). Define
\[
\rho_i=e_i^TG_i\mu_i,\qquad E_i=\mu_i^TG_i\mu_i,\qquad R=\sum_i\rho_i.
\]
Using the inverse formula with the block decomposition above,
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
=
4\sum_i E_i+\frac{(s+2R)^2}{S}.
\]

For each \(i\), the two vectors
\[
2\mu_i-e_i,\qquad 2\mu_i+e_i
\]
have odd coordinate at the root \(r_i\) and even coordinates elsewhere. By the induction hypothesis,
\[
(2\mu_i-e_i)^TG_i(2\mu_i-e_i)\ge\alpha_i,
\]
and
\[
(2\mu_i+e_i)^TG_i(2\mu_i+e_i)\ge\alpha_i.
\]
Expanding gives
\[
4E_i-4\rho_i+\alpha_i\ge\alpha_i,
\qquad
4E_i+4\rho_i+\alpha_i\ge\alpha_i.
\]
Therefore
\[
E_i\ge|\rho_i|.
\]
Thus
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
\ge
4\sum_i|\rho_i|+\frac{(s+2R)^2}{S}
\ge
4|R|+\frac{(s+2R)^2}{S}.
\]

It remains to prove
\[
4|R|+\frac{(s+2R)^2}{S}\ge\frac1S.
\]
Multiplying by \(S\ge1\), this is
\[
4S|R|+(s+2R)^2\ge1.
\]
Set \(y=2R\). Then this becomes
\[
2S|y|+(s+y)^2\ge1.
\]
If \(y=0\), this is \(s^2\ge1\), with equality exactly when \(s=\pm1\). If \(y\neq0\) and \(sy\ge0\), then \(|s+y|>|s|\ge1\), so the inequality is strict. If \(sy<0\), put \(h=|s|\ge1\) and \(u=|y|>0\). Then the left side is
\[
(h-u)^2+2Su.
\]
If \(h=1\), this equals
\[
1+u^2+2(S-1)u>1.
\]
If \(h\ge3\), then either \(u\le h-1\), in which case \((h-u)^2\ge1\), or \(u>h-1\), in which case \(2Su>2(h-1)\ge4\). Hence the inequality holds, and equality occurs only when \(R=0\) and \(s=\pm1\).

Therefore
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda\ge\frac1S=\alpha.
\]
If equality holds, then the scalar equality forces \(R=0\) and \(s=\pm1\). The exact formula then becomes
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda=4\sum_iE_i+\frac1S.
\]
Equality with \(\alpha=1/S\) forces every \(E_i=0\). Since each \(G_i\) is positive definite, every \(\mu_i=0\). Hence \(\lambda=\pm e_r\). Conversely, \(\lambda=\pm e_r\) plainly gives equality.

This proves the dual parity statement.

#### Derivation of the branch inequality

Let \(x\in\mathbb Z^C\), let \(c=x_r\), and let \(Q=x^TAx\). Fix \(k\in\mathbb Z\) and put
\[
t=2k-1.
\]
Define
\[
\lambda=2Ax-te_r.
\]
Because \(A\) is integral, \(\lambda\in\mathbb Z^C\). Its root coordinate is odd, and every nonroot coordinate is even. Therefore the dual parity statement gives
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda\ge\alpha.
\]
Expanding,
\[
\lambda^TG\lambda
=
(2Ax-te_r)^TG(2Ax-te_r)
=
4Q-4tc+t^2\alpha.
\]
Hence
\[
4Q-4tc+t^2\alpha\ge\alpha,
\]
so
\[
tc-Q\le\frac{t^2-1}{4}\alpha.
\]
Since \(t=2k-1\),
\[
\frac{t^2-1}{4}=k(k-1).
\]
Therefore
\[
(2k-1)c-Q\le k(k-1)\alpha.
\]

Equality holds if and only if \(\lambda=\pm e_r\). If \(\lambda=e_r\), then
\[
2Ax-(2k-1)e_r=e_r,
\]
so
\[
Ax=ke_r,\qquad x=kGe_r.
\]
If \(\lambda=-e_r\), then
\[
Ax=(k-1)e_r,\qquad x=(k-1)Ge_r.
\]
Thus equality occurs exactly when \(x=kGe_r\) or \(x=(k-1)Ge_r\), with the displayed vector integral.

#### Equality for \(k=0\) and \(k=1\)

Let
\[
y=Ge_r.
\]
For \(k=1\), equality implies
\[
x=0\quad\text{or}\quad x=y.
\]
For \(k=0\), equality implies
\[
x=0\quad\text{or}\quad x=-y.
\]
Thus nonzero equality for \(k=0\) or \(k=1\) forces \(y\in\mathbb Z^C\). Since \(y_r=\alpha\) and \(0<\alpha\le1\), integrality forces \(\alpha=1\).

We now show that \(\alpha=1\) forces \(C\) to contain a weight-one vertex. If \(C=\{r\}\), then \(\alpha=1/w(r)\), so \(\alpha=1\) gives \(w(r)=1\). Otherwise, using the child-branch notation above,
\[
\alpha=\frac1S,
\qquad
S=w(r)-\sum_i\alpha_i.
\]
Thus \(\alpha=1\) gives \(S=1\). But
\[
S=w(r)-\sum_i\alpha_i\ge w(r)-m\ge1.
\]
Therefore equality holds throughout. If \(m=0\), then \(w(r)=1\). If \(m>0\), equality forces every child branch to have \(\alpha_i=1\). Descending through a child branch and repeating, finiteness eventually leads to a one-vertex branch whose root has weight \(1\). Hence \(C\) contains a weight-one vertex.

Consequently, if \(C\) contains no weight-one vertex, then equality in the branch inequality for \(k=0\) or \(k=1\) occurs only when \(x=0\).

This completes the rooted branch lemma.

### 5. The global Schur complement inequality

Return to the original tree \(T\) and its unique bad vertex \(b\). Order the vertices by putting \(b\) first and then the vertices of the components \(C\in\mathcal C\). The full Gram matrix has block form
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
w(b)&-\eta^T\\
-\eta&B
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
B=\bigoplus_{C\in\mathcal C}A_C,
\]
where the \(C\)-component of \(\eta\) is \(e_{r_C}\). The Schur complement at \(b\) is
\[
w(b)-\eta^TB^{-1}\eta
=
w(b)-\sum_{C\in\mathcal C}\alpha_C.
\]
Because the full form is positive definite, this Schur complement is positive. Therefore
\[
\sum_{C\in\mathcal C}\alpha_C<w(b).
\]

### 6. Irreducibility of the unique bad vertex when there is no weight-one vertex

We prove that \(b\) is irreducible. It is enough to show that for every \(x\in L(T)\) with \(x\neq0\) and \(x\neq b\),
\[
x\cdot(b-x)<0.
\]
Indeed, if \(b=p+q\) with \(p,q\neq0\), then taking \(x=p\) gives \(q=b-x\), so \(x\neq0,b\).

Write
\[
x=kb+\sum_{C\in\mathcal C}x_C,
\]
where \(k\in\mathbb Z\) and \(x_C\in L(C)\). Let \(c_C\) be the coefficient of \(r_C\) in \(x_C\), and let
\[
Q_C=x_C^2
\]
computed using the form on \(C\). Since \(b\) is adjacent in \(C\) only to \(r_C\),
\[
b\cdot x=kw(b)-\sum_C c_C.
\]
Also, distinct components of \(T-\{b\}\) are orthogonal, so
\[
x^2=k^2w(b)-2k\sum_Cc_C+\sum_CQ_C.
\]
Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
x\cdot(b-x)
&=x\cdot b-x^2\\
&=
\left(kw(b)-\sum_Cc_C\right)
-
\left(k^2w(b)-2k\sum_Cc_C+\sum_CQ_C\right)\\
&=
k(1-k)w(b)
+
\sum_C\bigl((2k-1)c_C-Q_C\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]

Apply the rooted branch inequality to each component \(C\), with the same integer \(k\). Since each \(C\) is a rooted nonbad branch,
\[
(2k-1)c_C-Q_C\le k(k-1)\alpha_C.
\]
Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
x\cdot(b-x)
&\le k(1-k)w(b)+k(k-1)\sum_C\alpha_C\\
&=
k(k-1)\left(\sum_C\alpha_C-w(b)\right).
\end{aligned}
\]
But
\[
\sum_C\alpha_C-w(b)<0.
\]

If \(k\notin\{0,1\}\), then \(k(k-1)>0\), so
\[
x\cdot(b-x)<0.
\]

Now suppose \(k=0\) or \(k=1\). Then the upper bound above is \(0\), and each individual branch term satisfies
\[
(2k-1)c_C-Q_C\le0.
\]
If \(x\cdot(b-x)\ge0\), then every branch inequality must be an equality. Since \(T\) has no weight-one vertex, no component \(C\) contains a weight-one vertex. By the equality statement in the rooted branch lemma, equality for \(k=0\) or \(k=1\) forces
\[
x_C=0
\]
for every component \(C\).

Thus, if \(k=0\), then \(x=0\), and if \(k=1\), then \(x=b\). Both are excluded when \(x\neq0,b\). Therefore every \(x\neq0,b\) satisfies
\[
x\cdot(b-x)<0.
\]

Consequently, there are no nonzero \(p,q\in L(T)\) with
\[
b=p+q,\qquad p\cdot q\ge0.
\]
Thus \(b\) is irreducible. Combining this with the weight-one case, \(T\) always contains an irreducible vertex. \(\square\)
self_declared_problem_solved: Let T=(V,E,w) be a finite weighted tree, meaning that (V,E) is a finite connected acyclic graph and w:V->Z is an integer weight function. For v in V, let d_T(v) be its graph degree. Let L(T) be the free abelian group generated by V with symmetric bilinear form given by v·v=w(v), by u·v=-1 when u and v are adjacent, and by u·v=0 when u and v are distinct and nonadjacent. Assume the form is positive definite. Call a vertex bad if w(v)<d_T(v), and call y in L(T) irreducible if there are no nonzero p,q in L(T) with y=p+q and p·q>=0. If there is exactly one bad vertex, then T contains an irreducible vertex; more precisely, every vertex of weight 1 is irreducible, and if no vertex has weight 1, then the unique bad vertex is irreducible.
advisor_statement: Let T be a finite weighted tree with positive-definite lattice form. Suppose there is a unique vertex b such that w(b)<d_T(b). Then T contains a vertex that is irreducible in L(T); indeed, if T has a weight-one vertex then such a vertex is irreducible, and otherwise the unique bad vertex b is irreducible.

description: Search for a multi-bad all-reducible counterexample or prove a bounded no-go by parity enumeration
full_text:
I did **not** find an all-reducible counterexample. But the \(s=5\) resonance is real: there are explicit positive-definite rooted branches with \(c=2,\ Q=5,\ \alpha\in[4/5,5/6)\). The natural central construction using the first such branch is positive definite and makes the central bad vertex reducible, but it still has irreducible secondary bad vertices.

## 1. An explicit infinite family of \(s=5\) resonant branches

Let \(n\ge 8\) be even. Define a rooted branch \(B_n\) as follows.

Vertices:
\[
r,\ s,\ p_1,\dots,p_{2n-3},\ q.
\]

Edges:
\[
r-s,\qquad s-p_j\ (1\le j\le 2n-3),\qquad s-q.
\]

Weights:
\[
w(r)=\frac{n+2}{2},\qquad w(s)=2,\qquad w(p_j)=n,\qquad w(q)=n+1.
\]

Define
\[
X_n=2r+n s+\sum_{j=1}^{2n-3}p_j+q.
\]
Then the \(s\)-subbranch has norm
\[
(n s+\sum p_j+q)^2
=
2n^2+(2n-3)n+(n+1)-2n(2n-2)
=
2n+1.
\]
Therefore
\[
X_n^2=4w(r)+(2n+1)-4n
=2n+4+2n+1-4n=5.
\]
So \(X_n\) has root coefficient \(c=2\), norm \(Q=5\), and hence \(F=5c-Q=5\).

The inverse-root entry of the \(s\)-subbranch is
\[
\alpha_s
=
\frac{1}{2-\frac{2n-3}{n}-\frac1{n+1}}
=
\frac{n(n+1)}{2n+3}.
\]
Thus the inverse-root entry of \(B_n\) at \(r\) is
\[
\alpha_n
=
\frac{1}{\frac{n+2}{2}-\frac{n(n+1)}{2n+3}}
=
\frac{4n+6}{5n+6}.
\]
For \(n\ge8\),
\[
\frac45<\frac{4n+6}{5n+6}<\frac56.
\]
So these are genuine \(s=5\) resonant branches with all weights \(\ge2\).

The smallest member is \(n=8\):
\[
w(r)=5,\quad w(s)=2,
\]
with \(13\) leaves of weight \(8\) and one leaf of weight \(9\). For this branch,
\[
\alpha=\frac{19}{23}.
\]

## 2. The central \(s=5\) construction works at the central vertex

Use six copies of the \(n=8\) branch. Let the central vertex be \(u\), with
\[
w(u)=5,
\]
and attach \(u\) to the six branch roots \(r_1,\dots,r_6\).

For each branch, let
\[
X_i=2r_i+8s_i+\sum_{j=1}^{13}p_{ij}+q_i.
\]
Then
\[
X_i^2=5,\qquad \text{coefficient of }r_i\text{ in }X_i=2,
\]
and the branch inverse-root entry is \(19/23\).

Positive definiteness follows by Schur complements:
\[
5-6\cdot\frac{19}{23}=\frac1{23}>0.
\]
The central vertex is bad since \(w(u)=5<6=d(u)\).

Now set
\[
Y=5u+2\sum_{i=1}^6 X_i.
\]
Then \(Y\in u+2L(T)\), \(Y\ne\pm u\), and
\[
Y^2
=
25w(u)+\sum_{i=1}^6\bigl(4X_i^2+20\,u\cdot X_i\bigr).
\]
Since \(u\cdot X_i=-2\),
\[
Y^2
=
25\cdot5+6(20-40)=125-120=5=u^2.
\]
Thus the central vertex \(u\) is reducible.

So the \(s=5\) central resonance is not merely formal: it produces an exact positive-definite tree with a bad reducible central vertex.

## 3. But the secondary vertices \(s_i\) are irreducible

This tree is **not** an all-reducible counterexample. In fact, each secondary vertex \(s_i\) of weight \(2\) is irreducible.

Fix one \(s=s_i\). Removing \(s\), the components are:

- \(13\) leaves of weight \(8\);
- one leaf of weight \(9\);
- the \(r\)-side component.

For the \(r\)-side component, after deleting \(s\), the central side has inverse-root entry
\[
\alpha_u=\frac{1}{5-5\cdot 19/23}=\frac{23}{20}.
\]
Therefore the \(r\)-side inverse-root entry is
\[
\alpha_R
=
\frac{1}{5-\frac{23}{20}}
=
\frac{20}{77}.
\]

Now suppose
\[
Y=\sigma s+2Z\in s+2L(T),
\]
with \(\sigma>0\) odd. The condition \(Y^2\le s^2=2\) is equivalent to
\[
\sum_C(\sigma c_C-Q_C)\ge \frac{\sigma^2-1}{2},
\]
where \(c_C\) is the root coefficient of \(Z_C\) and \(Q_C=Z_C^2\).

For a leaf of weight \(b\), the largest possible contribution is
\[
M_b(\sigma)=\max_{k\in\mathbb Z}(\sigma k-bk^2).
\]
For the \(r\)-side component, Cauchy gives
\[
\sigma c-Q\le \frac{\alpha_R\sigma^2}{4}
=\frac{5\sigma^2}{77}.
\]
Hence
\[
\sum_C(\sigma c_C-Q_C)
\le
13M_8(\sigma)+M_9(\sigma)+\frac{5\sigma^2}{77}.
\]

For \(\sigma\ge23\),
\[
M_8(\sigma)\le\frac{\sigma^2}{32},\qquad
M_9(\sigma)\le\frac{\sigma^2}{36},
\]
so
\[
13M_8(\sigma)+M_9(\sigma)+\frac{5\sigma^2}{77}
\le
\left(\frac{13}{32}+\frac1{36}+\frac5{77}\right)\sigma^2
=
\left(\frac12-\frac{23}{22176}\right)\sigma^2
<
\frac{\sigma^2-1}{2}.
\]
The remaining odd values \(3\le\sigma\le21\) are checked exactly:

\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c}
\sigma & M_8(\sigma) & M_9(\sigma) &
13M_8+M_9+\frac{5\sigma^2}{77} &
\frac{\sigma^2-1}{2}\\
\hline
3&0&0&45/77&4\\
5&0&0&125/77&12\\
7&0&0&245/77&24\\
9&1&0&1406/77&40\\
11&3&2&3762/77&60\\
13&5&4&6158/77&84\\
15&7&6&8594/77&112\\
17&9&8&11070/77&144\\
19&11&10&13586/77&180\\
21&13&12&16142/77&220
\end{array}
\]

So no witness exists for \(\sigma\ge3\). For \(\sigma=1\), each leaf contribution \(c-Q\le0\), with equality only for the zero vector. The \(r\)-side also has \(c-Q\le0\), because \(\alpha_R<1\). Thus equality occurs only when \(Z=0\), giving \(Y=s\), which is excluded. Therefore \(s\) is irreducible.

So this central \(s=5\) construction fails to be all-reducible for a rigorous reason.

## 4. Meaningful obstruction proved

More generally, take any central tree built from copies of the \(n=8\) branch above, with central weight \(m\) and \(N\) branches. If
\[
m>\frac{19N}{23},
\]
then the tree is positive definite. If also
\[
6m\le5N,
\]
then the central vertex has the same \(s=5\) reducibility witness.

But every secondary vertex \(s_i\) is still irreducible. Indeed,
\[
m-\frac{19N}{23}\ge\frac1{23}
\]
by integrality, so after deleting one branch,
\[
\alpha_u\le\frac{23}{20},
\qquad
\alpha_R\le\frac{20}{77}.
\]
The same parity-coset estimate above applies.

Thus the first exact star-shaped \(s=5\) central construction cannot give an all-reducible counterexample.

---

\[

\[

\[
\frac45<\frac{4n+6}{5n+6}<\frac56.

\[

\[

and attach \(u\) to the six branch roots \(r_1,\dots,r_6\).
